documents.displayName: CI Cache Strategy for Branch-Pinned Swift Package Dependencies
documents.topic: ci/cache

Cross-ecosystem CI/CD re-use across the 17-org Swift Institute manifest. Six-question investigation, v1.1.0 with principal correction: (Q1) L2/L3 wrapper — MUST exist mirroring layer orgs (codified as [CI-004a]); v1.0.0's no-wrapper verdict reversed because the org hierarchy IS the architectural primitive and CI hierarchy mirrors it; create swift-standards/.github + swift-foundations/.github with thin-pass-through wrapper files initially. (Q2) 11 L2 spec-authority sub-orgs — 3-tier through LAYER wrapper (sub-org repos route via swift-standards or swift-foundations); per-authority wrappers DEFERRED, structurally blocked by GitHub Actions workflow_call 4-level depth limit (current L1 chain per-package→layer-wrapper→universal→advisory-linter is at the limit; adding sub-org wrapper would exceed); codify as [CI-004b] (sub-org wrappers are future work, sunsets when GitHub raises limit OR universal is refactored to inline advisory linters). (Q3) Cross-org bot + secret distribution — existing mechanism correct (per-org App install + central client-id pair + per-org PRIVATE_REPO_TOKEN per [CI-060]); add lint-org-bot-coverage.yml advisory linter. (Q4) Third-party re-use — unintended-but-permitted, disclaimer in universal header. (Q5) Bot's expanded role — current services scale via orgs.yaml filter expansion; no L2/L3-specific service clears [RES-018]; one new META service (lint-org-bot-coverage.yml) with THREE coverage axes (App install + org secret + NEW v1.1.0 wrapper-file presence). (Q6) Migration — THREE-axis (v1.0.0 was two-axis): (A) create L2+L3 layer wrappers, (B) migrate ~280 consumer per-package ci.yml callers to route through layer wrappers, (C) phased orchestrator-filter expansion; 11 phases plus future option (Phase 12+) for per-authority wrappers post-limit-relief. Most consequential finding: principal correction reframes question from 'what invariants justify a wrapper?' to 'what does org hierarchy require?'; layer wrappers exist unconditionally at every layer org as anchor for future accretion. 4-level workflow_call limit is a binding architectural constraint preventing full org-hierarchy mirroring at sub-org level today. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.1. Reviewer pass corrections: migration scope corrected to 262 (verified disk grep: L2 20 + L3 139 + L2 sub-orgs 101 + L3 sub-orgs 2; was '~280'); 4-level cap granularity nuance added (binds 6 advisory linter sub-dispatches only, not matrix/build/test); [RES-018] tension explicit (layer wrappers as no-op delegations on day 1 trade ~30–90 s setup overhead per run for zero future migration cost); L3 sub-orgs in CONSTRUCTION not MIGRATION (Phase 9 split into 9a/9b); lint-org-bot-coverage.yml auth-path designed (admin:org for axes 1+2, anonymous public-API for axis 3); Phase 12+ inline-linters trade-off requires costed analysis (linters also dispatched directly by cron orchestrators). v1.1.0 framing (CI hierarchy mirrors org hierarchy, principal correction) unchanged. Skill-rule extensions [CI-004a] + [CI-004b] proposed for next ci-cd-workflows skill update wave. Three-axis migration covers wrapper creation + caller migration (262 callers) + orchestrator filter expansion. New advisory reusable lint-org-bot-coverage.yml proposed as Phase 3 deliverable. No workflow YAML modified per Do-Not-Touch. Composes additively with project_pending_cicd_security_handoff.md. Companion HANDOFF deleted during /reflect-session bounded-cleanup-authority firing; Research doc is canonical.

documents.topic: Comparative prior-art survey of CI/CD patterns across 6 non-Swift ecosystems (Rust, Go, Python, JS/TS, multi-language large-OSS, plus Apple's swift_package_test reference). Cross-ecosystem synthesis identifies universal patterns (composite actions for setup recipes, scheduled-cron sweeps, harden-runner egress lockdown, OSSF Scorecard, OIDC keyless publishing), common patterns (workflow_call reuse, conclusion-aggregator job, hot-path/cold-path split, sharded testing, generated workflows from typed source), and convergent absences (layered package-tier organization, maintainer-operated cross-repo cron orchestrator, public/private visibility gate — all swift-institute-original design). Application to Swift Institute identifies 3 high-value candidate cohorts: (1) step-security/harden-runner egress lockdown (closes the supply-chain gap left by SHA-pinning declination); (2) Rust-style conclusion-aggregator job (decouples branch protection from workflow refactors); (3) OIDC keyless attestation for tagged releases via sigstore/cosign (establishes signing infrastructure ahead of SwiftPM Trusted Publishers). 3 deferred candidates (hot-path/cold-path split, swift-tools-version derivation, DockerHub→ghcr.io mirror) — analogs don't fit cleanly or trigger condition not yet present. 3 declined candidates (generated workflows from typed source, external CI, external dashboards) — appropriate at scales swift-institute hasn't reached. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.
documents.statusDetail: Survey produces 3 high-value candidate cohorts pending principal authorization for next-cohort sequencing

documents.topic: Comprehensive security review of the swift-institute + swift-primitives CI/CD stack across 5 scopes: (1) per-component matrix of every workflow / composite action / helper script / cron orchestrator with action SHA-pin state, secret scope, network egress surface, supply-chain dependencies, and token-leak surface; (2) threat model enumerating six attack paths labeled plausible-vs-speculative — T1 secrets exfil via fork-PR (closed by GitHub fork-secret rules + no `pull_request_target`), T2 write access via compromised reusable (closed by permissions intersection rule + branch protection IFF landed), T3 swift-institute-bot 17-org App-token escalation (chokepoint = branch protection on swift-institute/.github), T4 supply-chain via unpinned action / curl-piped helper / docker pull (T4a Dependabot for GitHub Actions partial mitigation noted), T5 shell-injection via repo-controlled content (mitigated by env-var indirection except in audit-step pattern which reduces to T2/T3 chokepoint), T6 speculative paths (cache poisoning, container escape); (3) gap inventory — 10 gaps spanning correctness / secret-leak / supply-chain / write-access-elevation classes, with G1 branch-protection identified as the single highest-priority remediation (collapses T2/T3/T5 to one chokepoint), G10 bot-installation-permissions audit added as NEW Cohort F; (4) branch-protection minimal proposal with exact `gh api -X PUT` shape per repo and three-wave provisioning order (host repos → 4 canaries → 128-repo fleet) including post-PUT diff-verification step + L2/L3 wrapper-host forward-reference, explicit per-action authorization gates per [CI-052]; (5) cohort sequencing producing 6-phase plan with 13 per-action authorization gates total over ~3 weeks. Most consequential finding: branch protection on swift-institute/.github + swift-primitives/.github is the single architectural chokepoint that closes the bot's 17-org write-elevation path; without it, anyone with push access to those two repos can mint contents:write tokens on any of 17 orgs via generate-metadata.yml. Top-level verdict: stack is in good security posture for its maturity (post Cohorts A1 + B Phase 1); remaining risk is dominantly chokepoint-resolvable. No new ecosystem primitives recommended ([RES-018] check); every cohort composes with existing infrastructure. Composes additively with ci-centralization-strategy.md (Path B / @main strategy), ci-cd-prior-art-and-pattern-survey.md (3 high-value candidates — 2 landed, 1 queued), and ci-cd-cross-ecosystem-reuse.md (lint-org-bot-coverage.yml extension noted; L2/L3 layer-wrapper repos join Wave 1 in next protection refresh). Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.0. Surgical revisions in response to independent-assessment pass (8 issues raised, all resolved without restructuring; no conclusions reversed). v1.1.0 additions: (a) §Scope 4 enforce_admins trade-off paragraph (default true on correctness>security>speed axis but principal explicitly chooses; emergency-fix paths route through temporary protection-disable + targeted push + re-enable); (b) §Scope 4 Wave 1 verification extended — gh api GET + diff against expected JSON to catch gh CLI parameter-encoding gotchas (--field vs --raw-field nested-object stringification); (c) §Scope 4 forward-reference to swift-standards/.github + swift-foundations/.github joining Wave 1 once the cross-ecosystem-reuse v1.1.0 wrapper cohort lands; (d) §Scope 3 NEW G10 (bot installation permissions audit) — Risk: write-access-elevation; Scope: fleet (17 orgs); Resolution: per-org `gh api orgs/<org>/installations/<id>` → manifest at read-orgs/bot-installations.yaml; NEW Cohort F parallel to Cohort D in Phase 3; ~1.5 hours; (e) NEW [CI-082] proposal — binary-install version-bump protocol (every version bump re-locks SHA-256 in the same PR; CI fails closed on digest mismatch); (f) §Scope 3 G7 generalization — [CI-081] proposed text now reads 'callers in any branch-protected layer-wrapper repo' so the rule survives the L2/L3 wrapper rollout; (g) §Scope 2 T4a Dependabot addendum — major-tag-creep mitigation, both .github repos already carry dependabot.yml; (h) §Scope 5 Phase 5 audit-log review cadence — quarterly gh api orgs/<org>/audit-log for protected_branch.policy_override + installation.create_token events, paired with G10's cadence so principal runs both checks in one sitting. Authorization-gate count 12 → 13 (Cohort F adds one). v1.0.0 framing unchanged: top-level finding is single chokepoint = branch protection on the two .github host repos; without it T2/T3/T5 collapse to 'push to main'. v1.0.0 baseline: comprehensive security review consuming H1–H4 reviewer feedback + Cohorts A1 (harden-runner audit-mode floor) + B Phase 1 (ci-ok aggregator) as input; sequenced 6-phase plan; branch-protection proposal with exact gh api PUT shape + three-wave order (host repos → 4 canaries → 128-repo fleet); skill amendments proposed for [CI-080] (block-mode-flip protocol detail) + new [CI-081] (audit-step trust-boundary pattern, generalized in v1.1.0) + new [CI-082] (binary-install version-bump protocol, added in v1.1.0). No workflow YAML modified; no remote pushed; this RECOMMENDATION is the sole artifact. Companion HANDOFF (HANDOFF-ci-cd-security-review.md) updated with Findings pointer to this doc.

documents.topic: CI/CD centralization strategy across ~300 repos in 11 orgs. Compares reusable workflows (current), repository rulesets (deprecation of required-workflows → rulesets successor; Team+ plan required), git subtree/submodule, and the sync-script model (established pattern — gitignore, community-health, dependabot). Recommendation: reusable workflows as foundational layer + sync-script for operational per-repo config. v1.1.0 adds Permission scoping section: Path B (per-caller explicit permissions) over Path A (org-level write default), with ecosystem-wide rollout outcome (87/87 public verified green; 210 private billing-blocked across 3 layers) and a durable private-repo billing-state note to prevent mistaking pre-existing blockers for Path B regressions. Empirical verification: swift-institute org on Free plan; rulesets gated; reusable-workflow permission intersection rule confirmed in execution. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.
documents.statusDetail: Phase 0/0b artifacts delivered; Phase II caller migration executed (297 callers, 11 orgs); Path B permission scoping rolled out 2026-04-22

documents.topic: Six-dimension read-only uniformity audit conducted post v1.1.1 rollout (2026-05-06) per principal directive. Verifies that the rollout's 'full uniformity' claim (scoped to ci.yml chain-routing, 0 stragglers) extends to the broader uniformity surface. D1 wrapper-file content: DELIBERATE-DIVERGENCE between L1 (5 layer-specific jobs per [CI-020]) and L2/L3 (thin pass-through per [CI-004a]); UNIFORM between L2 and L3 modulo layer-name strings + extensibility-hint comments. D2 caller routing: UNIFORM, 132+120+138=390 ci-routers and 390 docs-routers, zero drift. D3 metadata.yaml schema: UNIFORM, 390/390 with consistent description/topics/homepage three-key schema. D4 host-repo health files: UNINTENTIONAL-DRIFT — L2 + L3 hosts missing ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml + .swiftlint.yml + SECURITY.md vs L1 baseline; L3 also missing profile/README.md. Plus DELIBERATE-DIVERGENCE for swift-institute (umbrella role, runs Actions, hosts orchestrators/scripts/actions). D5 PRIVATE_REPO_TOKEN coverage: VERIFICATION-BLOCKED, my token lacks admin:org; defer to Sunday 2026-05-10 06:30 UTC lint-org-bot-coverage-weekly first cron fire. D6 sub-org wrapper presence: UNIFORM, 0/13 violations (correct per [CI-004b]). Counts: 3 UNIFORM, 1 DELIBERATE-DIVERGENCE primary + 1 partial, 1 UNINTENTIONAL-DRIFT, 1 VERIFICATION-BLOCKED. One remediation cohort proposed (Layer-host community-health sync, D4); one conditional cohort (App-permission-bump, D5 contingent on Sunday cron's axis-2 result). Stop conditions checked: D1 chain-routing-breaking — NOT triggered; D5 missing-secret — NOT triggered (cannot verify); >300 lines per dimension — NOT triggered. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Read-only six-dimension audit, no remediation. documents.topic: Architecture analysis of the Kuberwastaken/claurst specification documents (Tier 3) — eight specification documents reverse-engineered from the Claude Code TypeScript source (~1,902 files, ~800K LOC) into a Rust port. Catalogues query loop, tools system, agents, services, hooks, and special subsystems.
Implementation pattern analysis of Claurst, a Rust port of Claude Code (Tier 3, source as of 2026-04-01) — 11-crate Cargo workspace, decomposition patterns, dependency graph rooted at `cc-core`.

Clock Dependency.Key placement (Tier 2) — where to place `@Dependency(\.clock)` integration providing type-erased clock with mode-aware defaults (ContinuousClock production, Clock.Immediate tests). Requires access to both swift-dependencies (L3) and swift-clock-primitives (L1).

Clock.Continuous static now convenience and accuracy audit (Tier 2, v2.0) — 16 files using stdlib ContinuousClock.now. Three findings: (1) CRITICAL: Darwin uses `CLOCK_MONOTONIC` (buggy, can jump backwards) instead of stdlib's `CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW`; (2) add `static var now: Instant` in platform packages; (3) optional `clock_gettime_nsec_np` optimization. Performance analysis confirms `Self().now` matches stdlib.

DECISION (v1.0.0): Formalises the cohort's accepted [API-ERR-006] violation on Codable conformances. Either and Pair carry synthesized Codable conditional on Codable constraints (#if !hasFeature(Embedded) gated); Product carries manual init(from:)/encode(to:) with explicit throws(any Swift.Error). All three inherit untyped throws from the Codable protocol contract — the institute's [API-ERR-006] forbids untyped throws on public API. With Foundation Integration excluded ecosystem-wide (per feedback_improve_ecosystem_over_foundation_or_thirdparty + feedback_ecosystem_no_foundation_in_main_targets), the standard Foundation Integration subtarget escape hatch is foreclosed; dropping Codable was considered and rejected (loss of ecosystem compatibility). Disposition: ship Codable in main target; accept the violation as a permanent contingent trade-off until a typed-throws Codable lands in stdlib (analogous to SE-0499's Equatable upgrade). The violation does not propagate — consumers who wrap the cohort's Codable in their own typed-throws-bearing API can re-typify locally; the cohort is the boundary at which untyped throws stop. Documentation actions for Item C: each package README MUST carry a 'Codable conformance and typed throws' sub-section explaining the trade-off; swift-product-primitives/Audits/audit.md CS-2 SHOULD cite this disposition note.

Phase II.5 heritage-transfer plan for the 18-package coenttb/swift-html cluster. Three-stage framework (dep-visibility audit → disposition refinement → per-repo execution) gated by user constraint 2026-04-23 ("do not make things public yet"). Four dispositions per package: archive / transfer-simple / transfer-rename-and-reconcile / defer. Archive disposition uses deprecation-README + archive pattern (provenance: HANDOFF-swift-testing-successor-migration.md). Session 2026-04-23 landed 3 archives (types leaves superseded by body-org spec packages swift-whatwg-html, swift-w3c-css, swift-w3c-svg); 15 active of which 2 deferred (swift-renderable → swift-primitives TBD; swift-html-to-pdf needs refactor). Per-package status table + start-conditions checklist + Ground Rules 1–6 transcribed + swift-foundations sibling inventory (all 13 in-scope counterparts PRIVATE, 2 MISSING — universal dep-visibility gate applies) + verification stamp per [META-015]. Consolidates and supersedes HANDOFF-heritage-transfers-and-history-strategy.md per [META-016]. Cross-references git-history-transfer-patterns.md (recipes), github-organization-migration-swift-file-system.md (precedent), package-namespace-noun-convention.md (rename rationale), swift-translating-migration-plan.md (ecosystem-work invariants for Row 1 apply-on-top).

documents.topic: Per-paragraph evaluator-lens audit of the four swift-primitives 0.1.0 cohort READMEs (carrier, tagged, ownership, property). Diagnoses the recurring failure mode as **audience inversion** — author-side context (release-process state, design rationale, future-proposal speculation, internal workaround reasoning, ecosystem-convention framing) leaking into prose meant for consumer evaluation. Carrier post-cleanup is the discipline reference (control). Applies a KEEP / COMPRESS / RELOCATE / DELETE verdict per paragraph with line citations and concrete actions. Six surface patterns enumerated; canonical rewrites named per pattern. Per-repo cleanups land as separate follow-up commits ordered Property → Tagged → Ownership → Carrier (audit-only). Provenance: 3-round Claude/ChatGPT collaborative discussion CONVERGED 2026-05-01.

Keyed tree primitive design (Tier 3, v3.0) — SLR (34 sources: Fredkin, Hinze, Okasaki, Connelly-Morris, Bagwell, Prokopec), formal semantics, 5-option analysis. Existing tree types cannot be upgraded (child addressing baked into node layout). Literature: tries are maps, but swift-testing's Graph is a keyed tree (structural ops: subgraph, zip, recursivelyApply). `KeyedTree K V` subsumes `Trie K U` when `V = U?`. Recommends `Tree.Keyed<Key, Value>` in swift-tree-primitives.

RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.1): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide extraction of the cross-cutting structural finding from the just-closed swift-json v2 arc (value-tree-redesign-v2.md v1.1.0 SUPERSEDED-BY-EVIDENCE §12). The finding: Copyable value-wrappers + multi-buffer hash storage = refcount-per-copy cost dominates the algorithmic O(1) win at small N. The empirical record: the L1-1 prototype's storage swap from `[(String, Value)]` to `Dictionary<String, Value>.Ordered` regressed parse +339% and lookup +226% at the canonical workload (86 MB Swift.symbols.json, mean N=2.06) even though the isolated storage micro-bench (`crossover` mode) showed Dict.Ordered beating linear-scan 3.78×–192× at every N. A diagnostic Swift.Dictionary + side-order-array swap cut the regression roughly in half (parse +82%, lookup +159%) but still regressed materially — wrapper-indirection cost is dominant, storage-primitive choice is a 2× factor on top. The structural cost model: every wrapper copy increments one refcount per heap-backed component (1 for single-array, 2 for Swift.Dictionary + side array, 3-4 for Set.Ordered + Buffer.Linear + Hash.Table); a hot-path traversal of N wrappers with K heap-backed components pays N × K refcount operations. v1.0.1 softens three claims after first-reader review: (a) the integration-overstatement factor is scoped to the swift-json reference case rather than asserted as a general magnitude (the loose-end footnote always correctly classified it as a premise); (b) the N ≥ 30-50 break-even is relabelled back-of-envelope projection from §12's algebraic estimate — NOT empirically located, since the integrated bench was only run at the canonical workload's mean N=2.06 and never extended to large N; (c) Option B (break to ~Copyable wrapper) is clarified as theoretical-prediction-only — the v2 L1-1 prototype measured the *conditional*-Copyable L1 path, NOT the unconditional ~Copyable L2 path that Option B describes. The architectural fork stands: Option A (accept Copyable wrapper, keep single-array, accept O(n) — the swift-json default), Option B (break to ~Copyable wrapper, multi-buffer storage predicted-but-unmeasured), Option C (~Copyable companion in inline storage, e.g. Dictionary.Ordered.Small<N>). Decision rubric provided keyed on consumer's Copyable-requirement × median N. Methodological lesson promotable to a [BENCH-NNN] skill rule: isolated storage micro-benches MUST be paired with an integration probe through the consumer's actual access path before driving architecture decisions. Per [RES-019] internal grep: extends ecosystem-data-structures-inventory v1.0.0 + comparative-dictionary-primitives v1.0.0 + storage-primitives-comparative-analysis v1.1.0 + property-view-for-cow-copyable-types v2.0.0 + buffer-arena-conditional-copyable v1.1.0. Per [RES-021] contextualization step: cross-language analogues (Rust HashMap move-by-default; C++ std::unordered_map; Haskell Data.HashMap.Strict GHC-RTS-managed) do not exhibit the pattern because the wrapper-copy boundary that triggers it is Swift-specific. Per [RES-022] structural-correctness framing: refcount-cost model dominates over diff-size axis. Per [RES-023] empirical-claim verification at write time: every measured value cited against value-tree-redesign-v2.md file:line with verification date 2026-05-13.

When fixing a layering or structural violation in one per-platform package, sibling per-platform packages' working analogs are often the fastest refactor template. Origin instance: swift-windows.Thread.Affinity (2026-04-26 P3.5 refactor) used swift-linux.Thread.Affinity as structural template (NUMA discovery via same-package call + downward System_Primitives import). Doc scopes when the heuristic applies (cross-platform-shared concerns with shared conceptual model) vs when it does not (genuinely platform-divergent subsystems like Mach ports / io_uring / Job Objects, all-siblings-violate cases, platform-conditional rules). Open: ecosystem-wide census, failure modes, skill-level codification candidacy.

documents.displayName: Cursor Abstractions Across the Institute L1 Ecosystem
documents.topic: IMPLEMENTED (Shape γ) (v1.4.0, 2026-05-17): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide systematic-literature-review arc on cursor abstractions across the institute L1 ecosystem. Closed under Principal Authorization 2026-05-17 — choice C: Shape γ as the immediate move (Phases 0-3 — W2 unification only; W1/W3 stay in current packages) WITH Shape ι expansion explicitly scheduled as a committed Phase 4 follow-on arc (W1/W3 migrate to cursor-primitives). Phases 0-3 LANDED 2026-05-17 across swift-primitives/swift-cursor-primitives (new repo, commits 0f57273 + 2bd7800), swift-primitives/swift-binary-parser-primitives (commit 13e4dbf2), swift-primitives/swift-lexer-primitives (commit 575e4cb), and swift-institute/Experiments (BENCH-011 probe at cursor-span-bench-011, commit 3180870). Phase 0 BENCH-011 gate GREEN: Text path parity (Lexer.Scanner already used Tagged<Text, Ordinal>); Binary path 20-100× faster, attributed to legacy `public var position: Int` storage preventing @inlinable method optimization (root cause documented in §Implementation Notes). Phase 3 termination gate clean across 7 packages — workspace grep zero residuals on old shapes; ecosystem build green including swift-foundations/swift-json's 216 tests passing (load-bearing Lexer.Scanner consumer). API surface as implemented adds seek(to:) to the original peek/peek(at:)/advance/advance(by:)/consume/isAtEnd/count/position enumeration — class-(c) judgment within signature-latitude for parser-machine backtracking support. Count retrofit (Int → Tagged<Byte, Cardinal>) not in original brief; source-transparency verified by build + workspace grep (no external typed-arithmetic on view.count). Phase 4 Shape ι expansion remains pending — gated on HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 2 settling; sole technical fallback voiding ι (BENCH-011 unmitigable regression) did not fire. Inventory at HEAD 2026-05-17 surfaced six cursor-like primitives at L1: Binary.Bytes.Input (W3: Copyable+Sendable, [UInt8], Index<UInt8>), Binary.Cursor<Storage> (W1 rw: ~Copyable, Escapable, dual Index<Storage>), Binary.Reader<Storage> (W1 ro: ~Copyable, Escapable, single Index<Storage>), Binary.Bytes.Input.View (W2: now Cursor.Span<Byte> typealias post-Phase-2a), Lexer.Scanner (W2: now thin wrapper around Cursor.Span<Text> + Text.Location.Tracker overlay post-Phase-2b), Lexer.Pull.Stream<Tokens> (~Copyable & ~Escapable, wraps Lexer.Scanner). Four-axis decomposition (ownership × mutability × storage × position-type) identifies three structural clusters fixed by Swift's type system. Key finding: SE-0503 Accepted; Parser.Protocol declares associatedtype Input: ~Copyable & ~Escapable at swift-parser-primitives Parser.Parser.swift:90-95; ~10 institute protocols use the pattern; 2026-01-19 Lifetime Dependent Borrowed Cursors.md doc's structural-constraint claim is OBSOLETED.

The Shape ι 'committed expansion' framing in v1.3.0 §Principal Authorization is closed under the v1.2.0 single-generic architecture's changed ground (Ownership.Borrow.\`Protocol\` is the borrow-view contract, not the owned-storage contract). W3 was settled 2026-05-18 by typed-input-unification.md v1.0.0; W1 is now settled. HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 1's Phase 4 expansion is CLOSED. Seven structural arguments grounding W1 principled-refuse (see cursor-w1-expansion.md §Phase 2 §Rationale). v1.7.0 (2026-05-18): adds §Phase 4 W3 Simplification recording that HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 2 (D5 Binary.Bytes.Input vs Byte.Input unification) landed as typed-input-unification.md v1.0.0 DECISION. Refinement 1 (morning, v1.1.0 DECISION shape): two-generic Cursor<Storage, PositionTag> + Byte.Borrowed in swift-byte-primitives (commits c0e50aa + 9e0bd46), swift-cursor-primitives reshape (64717b2), binary-parser-primitives retarget (65bcdfd0), lexer-primitives retarget (25dddd1). Refinement 2 (afternoon, v1.2.0 IMPLEMENTED shape): single-generic Cursor<DomainTag: Ownership.Borrow.\`Protocol\` & ~Copyable> with storage derived as DomainTag.Borrowed. Call sites collapse Cursor<Byte.Borrowed, Byte>(span) → Cursor<Byte>(span). Implementation: swift-text-primitives 190fb64, swift-cursor-primitives b4dc49e, swift-binary-parser-primitives a6fbf075, swift-lexer-primitives 511d06e. documents.displayName: Cursor Shape A vs Three Worlds: Storage-as-Borrow-Carrier Re-Evaluation
documents.topic: IMPLEMENTED (v1.2.0, 2026-05-18): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide successor arc to cursor-abstractions-l1-ecosystem.md v1.4.0. Principal-authorized 2026-05-18: transition to Shape A as canonical L1 cursor architecture. The v1.4.0 doc's Shape A rejection reasoning ('Swift offers no way to make Escapable conditional on a generic parameter') is empirically false — Tagged precedent + cursor-shape-a-feasibility experiment (commit fa990a4, V1/V2/V5/V6/V7 CONFIRMED) refute the literal claim. Storage-as-borrow-carrier (Ownership.Borrow.\`Protocol\` framework from ownership-borrow-protocol-unification.md v1.0.0; Pattern 1 borrow-view per nested-view-vs-borrowed-naming.md v1.2.0) sidesteps the Mode-discriminator constraint entirely. The implementation arc landed in two same-day refinements: Refinement 1 (morning, v1.1.0 DECISION shape) = two-generic Cursor<Storage, PositionTag> with Byte.Borrowed Case B conformer landed across 4 packages. Refinement 2 (afternoon, v1.2.0 IMPLEMENTED shape) = single-generic Cursor<DomainTag: Ownership.Borrow.\`Protocol\` & ~Copyable> with storage derived as DomainTag.Borrowed — the two-generic shape was structurally redundant because the conformance already encoded the Storage choice. Added Text: Ownership.Borrow.\`Protocol\` with Borrowed = Byte.Borrowed (principled domain-identity statement — text storage IS bytes). Call sites collapse to Cursor<Byte>(span) and Cursor<Text>(...). BENCH-011 replay GREEN at parity across all 4 probes after both refinements (ratios 0.998-0.999); ecosystem build gate clean across 8 packages (~990 tests). W1/W3 (owned-storage Worlds) explicitly deferred — the protocol-bound shape forecloses 'one cursor type for all three Worlds' option A; Phase 4 dispatch decides between sibling owned-cursor type and broader protocol bound. §Implementation Outcomes documents both refinements with implementation SHAs.

documents.displayName: Cursor Phase 4 W1 Expansion — Principled Refuse
documents.topic: DECISION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-18): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide DECISION closing the Phase 4 W1 expansion question left open by cursor-shape-a-vs-three-worlds.md v1.2.0 IMPLEMENTED. Three candidate options enumerated by the dispatch brief: (a) sibling owned-cursor type (Cursor.Owned<Storage> + Cursor.Owned.ReaderWriter<Storage> in swift-cursor-primitives), (b) broader Cursor.Storage.\`Protocol\` bound, (c) principled refuse. Verdict: **Option (c) principled refuse**. Binary.Cursor<Storage> + Binary.Reader<Storage> stay in swift-binary-primitives; swift-cursor-primitives stays at Tier 6-8 hosting only the borrowed Span-cursor cohort. Phase 4 closes without source changes. Seven structural arguments: (1) the v1.2.0 single-generic architecture changed the design ground — the 2026-05-17 Shape ι commitment was scoped to the now-SUPERSEDED Three-Worlds shape; (2) W1 has no cross-domain duplication (intentional structural specialization within binary domain per [API-IMPL-008]/[API-IMPL-006]); (3) Item 2 / typed-input-unification precedent (W3 placed in byte-parser-primitives, not cursor-primitives) supports per-domain owned-cursor placement; (4) Ownership.Borrow.\`Protocol\` is the borrow-view contract, not the owned-storage contract — no parallel exists in the ecosystem; (5) nested-view-vs-borrowed-naming.md v1.3.0 Pattern 3 classifies cursors as stateful traversal, not borrow-projections; (6) Tier 13 elevation post-publication-irreversible (cursor-primitives would gain Memory.Contiguous.Protocol dep elevating Tier 6-8 → Tier 13); (7) [ARCH-LAYER-006] domain completeness determines L1 existence regardless of consumer count. Closes HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 1's Phase 4 expansion. Phase 0 prior-research grep CLEARED with 8 load-bearing prior artifacts cited-and-extended; no class-(c) ecosystem escalation triggered; no 4th option materialized.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0 (2026-05-18, DECISION). Closes Phase 4 W1 expansion via Option (c) principled refuse. No source changes; Binary.Cursor + Binary.Reader stay in swift-binary-primitives unchanged. cursor-primitives stays at Tier 6-8 with only Cursor<DomainTag>. Sibling to typed-input-unification.md v1.0.0 (W3 dissolution) — together close Phase 4 of cursor-abstractions-l1-ecosystem.md. Doc back-port: cursor-abstractions-l1-ecosystem.md v1.7.0 → v1.8.0 §Phase 4 W1 Resolution; cursor-shape-a-vs-three-worlds.md v1.2.0 → v1.3.0 §Phase 4 Resolution; HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 1 marked CLOSED.

documents.topic: Investigation of whether Decimal.Exponent / Precision / Payload should integrate with the Carrier ecosystem (Tagged refactor, Carrier conformance, or remain standalone). Empirical baseline: zero `Tagged<_, Decimal.*>` and zero `Carrier<Decimal.*>` usage across the ecosystem [RES-018]. Tagged refactor structurally blocked by: (a) naming convention — no domain-typed phantom Tag (the role-axes within Decimal don't have natural domain names; synthesized `*Tag` suffixes violate API-NAME conventions), (b) literal-conformance gating per tagged-literal-conformances-fresh-perspective.md (Strideable interaction reactivates the cross-domain footgun). Carrier<Int> conformance erases domain identity per [IMPL-001] (treating power-of-10 exponent arithmetic as generic Int erases the domain that motivates the type). Trivial-self-Carrier conformance is premature without second consumer. Recommendation: Decimal types remain standalone struct types — populate a third role-class (domain-specific value types) alongside witness protocols (Rec #6) and operator-ergonomics protocols (Rec #7). Proposes Recommendation #8 for capability-lift-pattern.md codifying this third class. Supersedes inventory v1.0.0's Phase 4 CAN-yes verdict.
documents.statusDetail: Resolves Phase 4 of the carrier-ecosystem migration. Decimal Tagged refactor cancelled; types remain standalone. Inventory v1.1.0 Phase 4 entry deferred-then-cancelled per this analysis. Codification queued: Recommendation #8 in capability-lift-pattern.md v1.3.0.

Recommends 3-phase: Phase 1 mode propagation (eliminates dual scope push, 2 files); Phase 2 protocol refinement (`Witness.Key: Dependency.Key`, resolves name shadowing, 3 files); Phase 3 L1 key bridge (full cross-store visibility, 4 files).

Developer tool package architecture (Tier 3) — where tools sit in five-layer architecture, manifest parsing strategy (dump-package JSON + Codable), formal dependency graph semantics, per-target classification algorithm (UNUSED/REDUNDANT/NEEDED), type architecture. Case study: redundant dependency detection. Prior art: 6 ecosystems surveyed (Rust, Node, Go, Swift, JVM, Python). REDUNDANT detection is novel — no existing tool in any ecosystem performs it.

documents.topic: DocC behavior with @_exported umbrella re-exports and the idiomatic multi-target documentation pattern

DocC renderer + CLI search capabilities on Xcode 26.4.1 toolchain, and Phase 2 merged-site strategy for swift-institute.org. Primary-source verified: sidebar "Filter" + QuickNavigation both title-only (`.exec(t.title)`); `IndexingRecord` full-text shape exists but only emitted by `--emit-digest`, and `docc merge` drops it (MergeAction.swift:52 TODO). Refines the 2026-05-05 prior-art survey's 'universal absence' finding: Rust's crater, Apple's swift-source-compat-suite, Haskell's Stackage, and GHC's head.hackage all replay the graph at toolchain level; the library-level pattern is hand-curated matrix jobs in upstream CI (tokio's test-hyper/test-quinn with [patch.crates-io], Vapor's test-providers matrix with swift package edit --path — both verified file:line). No off-the-shelf GitHub Action or SwiftPM-native feature exists. Recommends a new advisory reusable lint-downstream-impact.yml invoked from the universal swift-ci.yml (at the same chain level as lint-api-breakage, so 4-level workflow_call limit compatible), composed of: (a) cached dependency-graph manifest at swift-institute/.github with hourly cron + post-merge regeneration; (b) matrix-job reusable scoped to direct dependents by default with full-impact label opt-in for transitive closure; (c) class A/B/C/D classification mirroring lint-api-breakage with baseline-comparison discipline. Two extensions: nightly cron sweep for transitive coverage, and an optional swift-institute.org dashboard surface. documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.2.0, 2026-05-21): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide inventory + triage of all institute-owned public protocols against the typed-throws associatedtype = Never sibling-form pattern from the byte-discipline arc. v1.2.0 amendment: Arc 1 execution attempt 2026-05-21 surfaced a structural blocker — when `Ordinal: Ordinal.\`Protocol\`` (or `Color: Color.\`Protocol\``) is declared with the AT, Swift name-lookup resolves Ordinal.Error / Color.Error to the pre-existing nested enum at that namespace, NOT to the default Never. Byte avoided this by happening not to have a nested .Error enum (contingent). Both Arc 1 and Arc 2 BLOCKED pending the AT-naming question (Error vs Failure); spun off Tier 2 research `protocol-error-associatedtype-naming.md` v1.0.0 (RECOMMENDATION A.1: rename AT to Failure ecosystem-wide including byte-discipline back-port). Arc 1 execution reverted; clean baseline preserved; nothing landed in source. Two-pass methodology unchanged: Pass 1 enumerated ~290 public protocols across the three layers and identified ~30 in-scope candidates; Pass 2 applied six-verdict triage rubric. Two FITS verdicts hold structurally but execution depends on AT-naming resolution: Ordinal.\`Protocol\` (Cyclic.Group.Static.Element ALREADY has init `init(_ position: Ordinal) throws(Self.Error)` at Cyclic.Group.Static.Element.swift:56 + typed Error enum at Cyclic.Group.Static.Element.Error.swift:17); Color.\`Protocol\` (7 in-scope conformers in swift-color-standard, 2 with silent gamut-clipping; Color.Error already exists at Color.Error.swift:8). Nine ALREADY-typed protocols (Parser/Serializer/Coder/Command/Command.Schema.Visitor/Memory.Allocator/Lexer.Pull.Tokens/Formatter/Binary.ASCII.Serializable). Four format-Codable protocols DOES NOT FIT per family-Codable convention. Carrier.\`Protocol\` DOES NOT FIT — sibling-form discipline.

DECISION (v1.1.0): Should the cohort's functor surface admit ~Escapable arms? Verdict per package: (a) swift-either-primitives — extend non-closure methods (swapped, value(of:)) to ~Escapable arms TODAY with @_lifetime(copy either); defer closure-bearing methods (map/flatMap/fold) pending Gap A. (b) swift-pair-primitives — type-level ~Escapable upgrade required first; then non-closure methods extendable with same pattern. (c) swift-product-primitives — defer indefinitely; parameter packs don't admit each T: ~Copyable & ~Escapable. Empirical verification: swapped + ~Escapable arms WORKS with @_lifetime(copy either); map + closure on full ~Escapable arms FAILS (Gap A); mixed-arm map (one ~Escapable, one Copyable) WORKS. PairNE feasibility verified via /tmp/escapable-research probe target. Cross-references nonescapable-ecosystem-state.md (canonical state DECISION 2026-04-02 §6 rec 3) which already classifies Pair as a viable conditional-Escapable inline container.

Test expectation failure reporting bridge (Tier 2) — two problems: (1) `record(failing:...)` silently drops failures under Apple's Swift Testing runner (`Collector.current` is nil), (2) inline snapshot rewriter never fires (depends on `Test.Runner.postRunActions`). 4 failure-reporting options + 3 rewriter-lifecycle options evaluated. Recommends Option A (`#if canImport(Testing)` fallback with nil-collector guard) + Option R1 (`atexit` handler, lazily registered on first `State.register()`, safely composable with `postRunActions` via destructive `drain()`). Full recording-mode lifecycle verified.
Experiment revalidation inventory — BUG REPRODUCED/REFUTED experiments for retesting on toolchain upgrade. [META-019] Phase 7.

Expression decomposition for `#expect`/`#require` macros (Tier 2) — 4 options (full Apple mirror, operator overloads, minimal macro decomposition, hybrid phased). Recommends Option D (hybrid): Phase 1 binary operator decomposition using existing `Test.Expression.Value`/`Test.Expectation.Failure` types (zero primitives changes, ~8 files); Phase 2 extends to function calls, property access, casts. Typed throws [API-ERR-001], no Mirror. Closes #1 usability gap from comparative analysis.

When an Institute package's design materially derives from a non-owned external upstream Swift package (not transferable via gh api .../transfer), what is the right git/GitHub-level heritage shape? Compares five options (independent re-implementation, fork + publication squash, fork + orphan-replace, mirror-and-sync, transfer-not-applicable) across heritage signal, license-compliance discipline, divergence ergonomics, and composition with the Institute publication-squash workflow. Recommends Option 2 (Fork + publication-squash on top of fork point) as canonical for this case. Includes attribution + license obligations, divergence policy (no upstream merges), and concrete eight-step application to swift-tagged-primitives → fork of pointfreeco/swift-tagged. Complements coenttb-ecosystem-heritage-transfer-plan.md (owned-source case) and git-history-transfer-patterns.md (which contrasts transfer vs fork-then-merge but did not codify the fork case as first-class).
documents.statusDetail: Recommendation: fork-as-heritage (Option 2) for non-owned external upstream with material lineage + permissive license + community overlap. DECISION (v1.0.0): Formalises the cohort's accepted disposition that Pair (struct) and Either (enum) ship @frozen + ~Copyable + no custom deinit, permanently fixing them as movement vehicles rather than resource owners. The combination forecloses adding a custom deinit in any post-1.0 minor version (deinit addition is an ABI break under library evolution); rescinding @frozen is itself an ABI break. Rationale: layout stability is a feature for primitive-tier consumers; resource-bearing types belong in resource-owning packages (swift-file-system, swift-process) where ~Copyable + deinit + no @frozen is the active pattern; consumers who need lifecycle behavior on contained values invoke apply with a closure that closes them. Asymmetry: Product is NOT @frozen — parameter-pack types lack stable layout commitment, so the annotation is deferred until parameter-pack ~Copyable / ~Escapable lands in Swift. Documentation actions for Item C: DocC + README must include 'Lifecycle: movement, not management' note; the Pair README's descriptor example must be reframed (or replaced with a non-resource example) to avoid the lifecycle-ambiguity flag the forums-review simulation predicted (forums-review-objections-2026-05-09.md item 3, score 40.62).

Why does [REFL-009] not prevent HANDOFF-*.md accumulation at workspace root (26 files at /Users/coen/Developer/ as of 2026-04-30 despite the rule's MUST-delete-when-complete clause)? Five-hypothesis diagnosis: HYP1 /reflect-session not firing (PARTIAL — rule IS firing); HYP2 bounded-cleanup-authority orphan zone (CONFIRMED, dominant — most files are out-of-authority for every session); HYP3 F-classified preserved indefinitely (CONFIRMED for SGR-unverified-but-superseded sub-class); HYP4 G-classified lacks auto-transition (CONFIRMED, latent — Path X completion will surface 7-9 simultaneous candidates); HYP5 working-dir non-git-tracked amplifies preservation bias (CONFIRMED amplifier). Six options analyzed; recommendation is composition of B (cadence rule analog [META-022]) + F (dispatch-side predecessor retirement) + amendment to [REFL-009] adding stale-override clause. Three skill amendments proposed: [HANDOFF-038] HANDOFF Staleness Threshold, [HANDOFF-039] Predecessor Retirement at Dispatch, [REFL-009] amendment. DECISION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-18): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide consolidation closing HANDOFF.md Wave 4 Item 14 (F3). Reconciles four packages in the hex / base-N encoding surface — `swift-primitives/swift-binary-base-primitives` (L1 mechanism, SHIPPED v0.1.0), `swift-ietf/swift-rfc-4648` (L2 spec-literal, public, Phase 2 algorithm-delegation refactor queued), `swift-primitives/swift-ascii-{parser,serializer}-primitives` (ASCII.Hexadecimal.Parser/Serializer — integer literal parse/serialize, distinct from binary→hex encoding), `swift-primitives/swift-byte-primitives` (Byte value type; explicitly defers hex rendering to encoder packages in its Byte.swift doc comment). Verified ecosystem state 2026-05-18 — `swift-binary-base62-primitives` (the handoff's listed package) does NOT exist on disk; the legacy `swift-base62-primitives` was heritage-forked into `swift-binary-base-primitives` per Phase 3 archive. Five sub-decisions: (Q1) each operation gets a home — Binary.Base.`N` at L1, RFC_4648.* at L2, per-convention L2 packages for non-RFC alphabets (Bitcoin base58, Z85, Ascii85, RFC 1924), L3 unifier `swift-foundations/swift-binary-base` deferred per Phase 4; (Q2) NO `Byte.Base*` namespace — Binary and Byte are peer subjects per the byte-extraction arc's binary-stack audit (GREEN); (Q3) RFC_4648 spec-mirroring lives at L2 per [API-NAME-003], delegates algorithms to L1 per reconciliation Phase 2; (Q4) convention alphabets ship in L1 (base62 standard/gmp/inverted), spec-authority alphabets ship in per-spec L2 packages; (Q5) `ASCII.Hexadecimal.*` coexists with `Binary.Base.16.*` — different data shapes (integer vs byte array), different subjects per [API-NAME-001b]. No source-code changes authorized; this is a consolidation analysis. Cites two predecessor docs (binary-base-n-encoding-family-architecture.md v1.0.0, binary-base-n-rfc-4648-reconciliation.md v1.0.0) and the byte-extraction DECISION (byte-primitive-extraction-and-domain-naming.md v1.0.1). Skill-promotion candidate flagged but not promoted (backticked-digit nested-type pattern for closed numeric variant sets — already noted by predecessor architecture doc).

Swift's @_spi-in-@inlinable rule is a direct-reference rule, not a transitive-call-graph rule. Composing @inlinable through a public non-@inlinable boundary severs the cascade. Earlier (2026-04-20) inferential extension that treated propagation as transitive is corrected by this finding. Implications for cross-package consumer migration patterns, error-conversion layering, and overall API-layer design. documents.displayName: Integration-Shape vs Substrate Cost — Skill-Amendment Proposal
documents.topic: Typed-primitive substrate cost claims (e.g., [INFRA-003]'s 'compiles to identical assembly', [CONV-010]'s 'typed-arithmetic is zero-cost') apply at the substrate level — per-operation, in isolation. They do NOT bound integration-shape cost — where on the call graph the substrate operations fire. The streaming-deserialize regression arc demonstrated the gap concretely: T-1 moved position-work from cold (error) path to hot (per-byte / per-token) path; substrate-level zero-cost claim was satisfied; +25% measured regression still fired. Proposes three composing amendments: (1) clarifying note on [INFRA-003] body in existing-infrastructure skill; (2) new [BENCH-012]/[BENCH-011a] companion in benchmark skill extending integration-probe trigger to call-graph-shape migrations; (3) audit-skill checklist requiring zero-cost-prior citations to be paired with hot-path integration assertion. Each amendment is small; together they cover the failure mode at three points (substrate citation, measurement gate, audit gate).

Whether RFC_791.IPv4.Address / RFC_4291.IPv6.Address should change internal storage to match POSIX in_addr / in6_addr for zero-copy unification with sockaddr wrappers. Empirical experiment (swift-rfc-4291/Experiments/ipv6-address-alignment) measured current layouts; prior-art survey (Rust, Go, .NET, Python) shows universal rejection of layout compatibility — Rust explicitly abandoned C sockaddr layout in PR #78802. Recommendation: keep host-order storage, fix inaccurate docstrings, compose at API boundary (iso-9945 / windows-standard accept RFC values, marshal into native sockaddr). Motivated by swift-kernel Finding #17 Windows gap.

When does an L3 domain package need its own cross-platform unifier surface (e.g., swift-sockets, swift-io) vs consuming swift-kernel directly (e.g., swift-file-system, swift-paths)? Operational rule: domain L3 needs own unifier when introducing public types distinct from kernel primitives, public operations composing kernel + domain policy, or spec deps belonging outside swift-kernel. documents.topic: Layout.Stack adaptation in pdf-rendering (Tier 2) — VStack/HStack typealiases to same type cause footgun. First-principles analysis: axis is data, diagonals correctly unrepresentable, spatial names belong in layout-primitives. Decision: `PDF.Stack(.horizontal) { }` with `.horizontal`/`.vertical` on `Axis<2>` in layout-primitives, default `.vertical`, remove VStack/HStack typealiases.

Decision rationale for deleting `swift-lifetime-primitives` from the workspace. Proposes the three-criteria primitive-rent test (capability beyond language + existing primitives, real consumer, theoretical content per [MOD-DOMAIN]). Per-type analysis: `Lifetime.Lease` redundant with `Ownership.Unique` (weaker semantics, runtime trap vs compile-time linear); `Lifetime.Disposable` symbolic protocol with no conformers; `Lifetime.Scoped` is `defer` in struct form. Documents the mechanical deletion (workspace files, 5 packages with commented-out scaffolding deps stripped). Cross-references the second-arc reflection.

documents.topic: Line box geometry unification (Tier 2) — duplicated half-leading computation in `CSS.LineBox` (swift-pdf-html-rendering) and `Style.Resolved.Line` (swift-pdf-rendering). 4 options: layout-primitives, text-primitives, new typography-primitives, status quo. Recommends `Layout.Line.Box` in layout-primitives — zero dependency cost, clear namespace, raw-height init (no font-metric coupling). Half-leading is CSS-specific but the geometric operation is general. `belowBaseline` stored, `aboveBaseline` replaces ambiguous `baselineOffset`. Cap-height centering identified as secondary unification candidate.

Sub-classification of the verification-taxonomy mechanical-bucket rules that apply to swift-primitives Swift code into four enforcement-tool buckets: swift-format / swiftlint-canonical / swiftlint-custom / custom-ci. Inputs read in full at write time: pilot + tier-1 + tier-2 + tier-3 classification docs (195 unique mechanical rules across 15 in-scope skills — code-surface 15 + primitives 4 + documentation 32 + existing-infrastructure 13 + swift-package 5 + swift-package-build 6 + conversions 18 + index 6 unique + memory-arithmetic 8 + memory-safety 27 + modularization 13 + platform 22 + swift-package-heritage 2 + testing 15 + testing-institute 9), swift-primitives root .swift-format (1907-byte JSON; 20 active swift-format rules + 14 disabled including ValidateDocumentationComments), canonical SwiftLint config at swift-standards/swift-standards/main/.swiftlint.yml (17 disabled + 9 opt-in + function_parameter_count tuning), Apple swift-format Rule Documentation (closed catalog of 43 rules: 18 linter + 25 formatter), SwiftLint Rule Directory (closed catalog of 324 rules: 129 default + 190 opt-in + 5 analyzer). Aggregate distribution: 1 swift-format (0.5%) + 1 swiftlint-canonical (0.5%) + 110 swiftlint-custom (56.4%) + 83 custom-ci (42.6%). Already-enforced count: 0 (the existing swift-primitives .swift-format active rules + canonical SwiftLint opt-in rules cover orthogonal formatting concerns; zero mechanical-bucket overlap with the in-scope set). Config-flips: 2 cheapest-path rules — [API-IMPL-005] One Type Per File via swiftlint-canonical one_declaration_per_file opt-in flip (single canonical-config edit; no fanout), [DOC-003] Method documentation via swift-format ValidateDocumentationComments flip from false to true (Fanout-Required: 133 sub-package .swift-format copies). Custom-rule-author cost characterization across 110 swiftlint-custom rules: ~25 one-liner regex (cheap, e.g., [API-ERR-001] typed-throws regex, [INFRA-020] Int(bitPattern:) trigger, [PRIM-FOUND-001] Foundation imports, [PLAT-ARCH-008c] L1 #if-os ban), ~55 moderate AST queries (e.g., [API-ERR-002] nested error types, [MEM-COPY-001] ~Copyable decl-site, [IDX-006] operator overload signatures), ~30 complex multi-decl / multi-rule clusters (e.g., [INFRA-200] operations-intentionally-missing closed pattern list, [CONV-003-006] API-table multi-pattern, [PKG-NAME-002/006] namespace+protocol+typealias triplets, [MEM-REF-002-005]/[MEM-LIFE-002-007] reference+lifetime primitive site enforcement). Custom-CI residual concentrated in: 27 Package.swift parsing (most of modularization + swift-package-build Linux family + [PRIM-ARCH-001/002] + nested-test-package rules), 30 DocC markdown / .docc file parsing (essentially all of documentation except [DOC-003] Swift doc-comments and [DOC-045] Swift // WORKAROUND: comments), 14 cross-package grep / cross-repo enumeration (most of platform PLAT-ARCH-* family), 5 build/shell invocations, 2 git-log/dependabot, 2 pre-commit hooks, 1 workflow-grep, 2 diff-aware predicates ([API-IMPL-010] visibility-widening trigger + [PKG-NAME-008] post-rename shadow scan). γ-roadmap migration candidates in scope: 1 — [PRIM-FOUND-001] No Foundation Imports = γ-1a deterministic in v1.2.0 §3.4.10, currently implemented as workflow YAML, could migrate to a one-liner SwiftLint custom rule in canonical .swiftlint.yml (parent_config: distribution removes per-package fanout cost); below the 5-rule cluster threshold, no coordinated migration dispatch warranted. γ-1b license-header is OUT OF SCOPE for this dispatch (readme skill excluded). No 5th-bucket (swiftsyntax-tool) candidates surfaced — every rule routed cleanly into the four buckets. Phasing recommendation (informational): Phase 1 config-flips (3 rules total counting γ-1a migration; minutes-to-hours), Phase 2 one-liner regex custom rules (~25 rules, low cost), Phase 3 moderate AST custom rules (~55 rules), Phase 4 complex multi-decl custom rules (~30 rules), Phase 5 custom-CI for Package.swift / cross-package / DocC / build-shape rules (~83 rules). Out of scope per supervisor block: any config file modification, PR drafts, workflow YAML drafts, γ-1a migration execution, fanout-mechanism work, generalization beyond swift-primitives, mechanical/hybrid/semantic re-classification.

Distribution heavily favors swiftlint-custom (56.4%) reflecting that most in-scope rules are AST/regex on Swift source; custom-ci (42.6%) concentrates in Package.swift parse, DocC markdown, cross-package grep, build/shell. Config-flip count 2 = strict-win immediate-coverage path. γ-roadmap migration candidate count 1 (PRIM-FOUND-001 = γ-1a Foundation, suspected migration to swiftlint-custom). No 5th bucket needed. documents.displayName: Memory, Buffer & Allocator: Institute vs. Apple/Swiftlang — Comparative Analysis
documents.topic: ANALYSIS (v1.0.0, 2026-05-31): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide. ROUND 2 of 2 — comparative analysis of the Institute's memory/buffer/allocator approach vs apple/swiftlang (Round 1 = apple-swiftlang-memory-buffer-allocator-survey + apple-swiftlang-arena-allocator-family-implementations). THESIS: shared SUBSTRATE (Institute deliberately REUSES stdlib Span/InlineArray/Synchronization/@_rawLayout/experimental @_lifetime; no swift-span-primitives; Array.Inline=typealias to Swift.InlineArray; atomics via stdlib AtomicRepresentable) + divergent SURFACE (typed/layered/namespaced/Foundation-free wrappers stdlib omits: Memory.Address integer-addressing, Storage.Protocol, Buffer.*, Ownership.* 7-type family, Memory.Arena/Pool/Allocator) + divergent MISSION (Foundation-independence hard+lint-enforced; mandatory strict-memory-safety + //SAFETY: disclosure vs apple opt-in SE-0458; typed-everything; Nest.Name+spec-mirroring; Byte/UInt8 split). SHARPEST DIVERGENCE: Institute SHIPS a public composable L1 arena/pool/allocator vocabulary incl a public Memory.Allocator.Protocol + a generational-slotmap arena (Storage/Buffer.Arena + Position handle = Rust slotmap idiom) apple has NOWHERE -- BUT the allocator protocol is DORMANT (1 conformer=system allocator, 0 consumers, arenas don't conform). [RES-021] contextualized BOTH directions: apple's no-public-allocator = deliberate, NOT a gap; Institute's dormant protocol = mild premature-surface smell (decision point). Maturity-fair: both ride the SAME experimental @_lifetime; Institute Ownership.Borrow/Inout are pre-SE-0519 stand-ins. Per-axis verdicts: Span=CONVERGE(reuse); ownership/inline/sync=CONVERGE+EXTEND; buffer-storage idiom=CONVERGE(value-facade+CoW)+EXTEND; arenas=DIVERGE; philosophy=DIVERGE(policy). OPEN DECISION POINTS (principal): wire-or-reserve Memory.Allocator.Protocol; document generational-arena as differentiator/SE-pitch; OutputSpan construction stance; naming convergence (stdlib-naming-beats-ecosystem-naming still IN_PROGRESS). Appendix B catalogs ~9 stale Institute docs found via [RES-013a]/[RES-023] verification (corpus-health, route to reflections-processing). Does NOT unilaterally mandate changes (principal convergence).
documents.statusDetail: Round 2 of 2 comparative (Institute vs apple/swiftlang); surfaces decision points for principal convergence, no unilateral mandates.

Optional<Copyable & ~Escapable> works in Swift 6.3 for computed properties, functions, and coroutines. Key insight: blocker is ~Copyable+Optional (consumption), NOT ~Escapable+Optional (lifetime). 7/7 experiment variants CONFIRMED. SE-0519 `Borrow<T>` converges on same design. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

Verdict: YES via @_owned + consuming get + UnderscoreOwned, BUT structurally blocked for generic ~Copyable enums (Either's exact shape) on the latest 2026-05-07-a nightly post the 2026-04-30 fix wave. Non-generic ~Copyable types work. Phase 1 ships today: free function `value(of:)` for ~Copyable arms; property form `e.value` retained for Copyable arms. ~Copyable value-generic deinit bug (Tier 2) — Swift compiler (#86652 variant) fails to synthesize member destruction for ~Copyable structs with cross-package, value-generic, @_rawLayout stored properties. Two sub-bugs: (A) deinit body skipped without reference-typed property, (B) automatic member destruction not synthesized. Workaround: `AnyObject?` + manual `remove.all()` via mutable pointer. Applied to 21 types across 9 packages. Simplified reproduction does not trigger (requires @_rawLayout).

Nonsending compiler patterns (Tier 2, v1.0.0) — Discovery: how the Swift compiler/stdlib uses nonisolated(nonsending). Key finding: stdlib deprecated `isolation:` parameter in favor of `nonisolated(nonsending)` on functions. Our `Async.Callback.callAsFunction(isolation:)` uses the deprecated pattern. Also: `sending` return types, no `@concurrent` in stdlib, implicit actor parameter at SIL level, conformance trap evidence.

Nonsending ecosystem migration audit (Tier 2, v1.0.0) — 26 `isolation:` parameter occurrences across ecosystem. 10 are SE-0421 conformances (KEEP). 14 are deprecated convenience functions (MIGRATE). Double-nonsending pattern applies to 8 witness/dependency functions. All 252 packages have NonisolatedNonsendingByDefault. All 7 AsyncIteratorProtocol + 9 Clock conformances pre-protected.

documents.topic: Resolves the Phase 2b migration question of whether to phase out per-type capability protocols (Cardinal.`Protocol`, Ordinal.`Protocol`, Affine.Discrete.Vector.`Protocol`) in favor of `Carrier<Underlying>`. Identifies an asymmetry: Cardinal and Affine.Discrete.Vector's operators are Self+Self-shaped (clean Carrier migration); Ordinal.`Protocol`'s typed-advance is Self+Self.Count where Count is per-conformer (Cardinal for bare Ordinal, Tagged<Tag, Cardinal> for Tagged) — Carrier has no Count associatedtype to replicate this. Enumerates seven options (Self.Domain-derived typealias, Carrier refinement, per-type extension, call-site migration, operator overloads, full retention, selective retention). Recommends Option G — Selective Retention: migrate Cardinal and Affine.Discrete.Vector to Carrier extensions (clean), retain Ordinal.`Protocol` as a SIBLING to Carrier (not a refinement, no V2 cost) for typed-advance ergonomics. Cites Recommendation #6 sibling precedent (Hash/Equation/Comparison coexist with Carrier). Articulates a third role beyond capability-lift and witness: `operator-ergonomics protocols` whose role is associatedtype-driven operator dispatch that Carrier's parameterized form cannot replicate. Cross-language prior art: Rust std::ops::Add, Haskell TypeFamilies, Scala dependent types — universal per-impl associated-type pattern.
documents.statusDetail: Implemented 2026-04-26. Cardinal/Affine.Discrete.Vector migrated to Carrier (clean); Ordinal.`Protocol` restored as sibling. Build + 35 ordinal tests + downstream packages green. Recommendation codified as Recommendation #7 in capability-lift-pattern.md v1.2.0.

documents.topic: Parameter ordering conventions for init and function signatures: closure-last rule, SE-0279 multi-closure lifecycle order, configuration-first-or-last (never middle), struct-over-builder-closure. Four rules promoted to code-surface as [API-IMPL-012] – [API-IMPL-015]. Synthesises Apple API Design Guidelines, SE-0279/SE-0286/SE-0245, TSPL, community writing, stdlib precedents, and zero-violation ecosystem survey. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

Parser bridge architecture (Tier 2) — bifurcated parsing in standards packages: composed parser combinators in Parse struct files vs ad-hoc byte-level parsing in public APIs. Zero public APIs delegate to parse structs. Investigation of bridge pattern to connect them.

Parser bridge ergonomics assessment (Tier 2) — MediaType exemplar validates parser bridge pattern. 6 friction points identified: ByteInput qualification noise (F-1), dual generic constraint `Collection.Slice.Protocol & Swift.Collection` (F-2, resolved by `Input.Slice: Swift.Collection` infrastructure fix), slice→String conversion ceremony (F-3), missing grammar combinators for `_splitOnComma`/`_trimOWS` (F-4), parser name collision (F-5), dead `_quotedString` helper (F-6). 313 lines of inline parsing across 10 unmigrated domain types. ISO 8601 demonstrates target unified pattern. 5 recommendations: migrate remaining types, add `TokenOrQuotedString` combinator, consider UTF8String wrapper (deferred), delete helpers post-migration, document constraint guidance.

Parser combinator algebraic foundations (Tier 3) — SLR (19 sources, Chomsky–Krishnaswami lineage), formal semantics, two-level algebra analysis (constructor-level vs value-level). Recommends Option D (doc + law tests) over explicit algebra dependency; identifies semiring-parameterized parsing as future work.

Parser syntax and ergonomics comparison (Tier 2) — how Swift Institute parser composition syntax compares to pointfreeco/swift-parsing in ergonomics, readability, and boilerplate. Architecturally stronger (typed throws, ~Copyable, generic Input) but syntactically heavier. Gap largest in error handling, parser definition boilerplate, ad-hoc parsing.

Parser combinator adoption implementation plan (Tier 2) — 8-phase leaf-first execution plan. Phase 0: Layer 1 ASCII integer parser (prerequisite). Phase 1: RFC 9110 shared HTTP parsers (8 reusable types). Phase 3: RFC 3986 URI sub-parsers. ~51 new files, ~67 rewritten files, ~123 parse functions affected. Foundation elimination for RFC 6750, W3C SVG, RFC 9111 CharacterSet leak. Phases 5–7 parallelizable.

Parser combinator adoption audit (Tier 1) — 95 adoption opportunities (52 HIGH) across ~30 standards packages. HTTP headers largest single opportunity (23 findings). ISO 8601 most impactful single rewrite (~400 lines). Foundation elimination possible for RFC 6750, W3C SVG, RFC 9111. Only 2 of ~100+ standards packages currently use parser-primitives.

Parsing and serialization capability organization from first principles (Tier 2, v1.3) — three fundamental capabilities (parsing, serialization, printing) plus one conjunction (coding/ParserPrinter). Three complementary implementation strategies: capability protocols, witness types, domain protocols. Domain namespace principle. Layer distribution. All tensions and open items resolved by transformation-domain-architecture (DECISION), canonical-witness-capability-attachment (DECISION), and ascii-serialization-migration (DECISION).

Coercible, Tov-Pucella affine types), formal semantics, cross-language comparison (Haskell/Rust/OCaml/TypeScript), Cognitive Dimensions. Validates Tagged design; identifies Swift's noncopyable phantom types as novel contribution.

RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0, 2026-06-01): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide. Correct suppressed-protocol bound for a PHANTOM (never-stored, discriminator-only) generic type parameter — settling the Escapable axis the literature study left open (it analysed only Copyable). Judged SOLELY on type-system first-principles (consumer/demand explicitly excluded per [ARCH-LAYER-008]/feedback_correctness_and_evergreen). VERDICT (determinate): a phantom parameter MUST be bound `~Copyable & ~Escapable` (maximal suppression); a non-suppressed marker requirement on a phantom is vacuous over-constraint (Reynolds parametricity — the implementation witnesses no capability of a phantom). Empirical (Verified 2026-06-01): three-regime drift — Tagged `Tag: ~Copyable & ~Escapable` (Tagged.swift:55, maximal) vs Index `Element: ~Copyable` (Index.swift:38, Escapable-required) vs Property `Tag` (Property.swift:46, both required); every Tagged capability conformance keys on Underlying NOT Tag (Copyable:115/Escapable:116/Sendable:126/Comparable:156), so the whole core operation surface already admits ~Escapable tags. RESOLVES the brief's UNVERIFIED item: relaxing Index.swift:38 alone is SUFFICIENT not hollow for ==/</retag/map. swiftc 6.3.2 probe: proposed `~Copyable & ~Escapable` Index admits a ~Escapable phantom tag, all ops available, result still Escapable (keys on Ordinal); current `~Copyable` bound REJECTS it (`NonEscTag does not conform to Escapable`). Generalises to Index/.Count/.Offset, Property<Tag>, per-container *.Indexed<Tag>, future markers. BOUNDARY: stored-value `~Copyable` params (Queue/Array/Stack) are OUT of scope — a separate value-semantics question. EXTENDS phantom-typed-value-wrappers-literature-study (Copyable axis → Escapable axis); a-fortiori successor to collection-index-escapable-consumer-fallout (that doc's VALUE-index DECISION admits ~Escapable even where escapability matters; a phantom never a value admits it more strongly). Proposed convention: new code-surface rule (sibling to [API-NAME-010/010a]) + conversions [IDX-001] update, via skill-lifecycle. Concrete diff documented (Index.swift:38 + parallel phantom sites across ≥5 packages + DocC) but NOT applied — coordinated cascade awaiting authorization + per-package build verification per [RES-023]/[feedback_no_redundant_long_builds].

RECOMMENDATION (v1.1.0, 2026-05-21): Tier 2 cross-package research closing the AT-naming question for typed-throws on capability-marker protocols. Triggered by Arc 1 execution attempt on 2026-05-21 (per ecosystem-associatedtype-error-inventory.md v1.1.0) hitting a structural blocker: when `Ordinal: Ordinal.\`Protocol\`` (or `Color: Color.\`Protocol\``) is declared with `associatedtype Error: Swift.Error = Never`, Swift's name lookup resolves `Ordinal.Error` / `Color.Error` to the pre-existing nested enum at that namespace, NOT to the default Never. The byte-discipline arc avoided this only because Byte happened not to have a nested Error enum (contingent, not structural). Five options evaluated; A.1 RECOMMENDED: A.1 rename AT to Failure ecosystem-wide including byte-discipline back-port (aligns institute with Parser/Serializer/Coder/Command + stdlib Result/AsyncSequence; ~5-8 files cost on byte-primitives + ascii-primitives); A.2 leave byte-discipline as Error, new protocols use Failure (bifurcation rejected); B rename each conformer's pre-existing nested Error enum to free Error for the AT (cascade per-protocol, doesn't generalize); C accept nested Error as the AT witness (loses universal-conformer ergonomics, rejected); D bifurcate per-protocol (convention drift, rejected); E `@_implements` escape hatch from blog `2026-04-20-associated-type-trap.md` empirically tested 2026-05-21 in /tmp/at-implements-test/ and REFUTED — the blog's two-stamp pattern handles two-protocol AT unification (HTML.Document conforming to both Rendering.View + SwiftUI.View), not the single-protocol nested-type default-override case; compiler reports 'multiple matching types named Error' and still resolves Self.Error to the nested enum (`Self.Error = Ordinal.Error`). Recommended execution: Wave 0 skill update (byte-discipline + code-surface rules), Wave 1 byte-discipline rename in swift-byte-primitives + swift-ascii-primitives, Wave 2 Ordinal arc, Wave 3 Color arc. Three open questions for principal: OQ1 skill update mechanical-only check, OQ2 refined-conformer wiring shape, OQ3 byte-discipline contingent-absence reasoning. Composes with: byte-protocol-capability-marker.md v1.1.0 (Q1 anchor), byte-arithmetic-conformance.md v1.0.0 (Q3 anchor), ecosystem-associatedtype-error-inventory.md v1.2.0 (parent inventory; Q1+Q2 subordinate to this doc), Blog/2026-04-20-associated-type-trap.md (Option E hypothesis source).

documents.topic: Recovers on-par-or-better performance for the Round-1 + Round-2 result-builder ecosystem when builder bodies contain `for` loops or bulk sequences. Empirical baseline (`Experiments/result-builder-perf/`) showed 11-39x slowdown vs imperative for `Swift.Array` for-loop bodies at N=100-1000. Root cause: per-iteration `[Element]` allocation in `buildExpression`, mandated by SE-0289's transform of `for` in builder body. Final decision (v2.0.0): ship Option G — bare `Swift.Sequence` overload added as `buildExpression<S: Sequence>(_:) where S.Element == Element`. An OVERLOAD, not a TYPE — satisfies the no-new-type constraint while delivering 0.13x of imperative for N=100 (8x faster) and 0.17x for N=1000 (6x faster). Combined with Option B (consume + append in buildPartialBlock), removes O(N) array-concat for mixed bodies. The `Repeat<S, Element>` helper (Option E) was prototyped, validated, and rejected in favor of Option G — identical performance, no new type, cleaner ergonomics. For-loop pattern documented as anti-pattern; users write `0..<N` directly.
documents.statusDetail: v2.1.0. The 'in-body .map is 21x slower' loose end filed in v2.0.0 has been investigated and REFUTED (see Experiments/result-builder-map-investigation/). The slowdown is not a builder anomaly — same cost inside or outside builder bodies. The v2.0.0 decision (Option G + Option B) stands. v1.0.0 recommended Option E (Repeat helper); v2.0.0 supersedes after the user constraint 'we'd prefer NOT adding a type in standard-library-extensions' + 'we control all packages, can change all code' produced the Option G + Option B path. Implementation shipped: standard-library-extensions builders (Array, ContiguousArray, ArraySlice, Set, Dictionary) + 13 institute Round-1/Round-2 builders. Empirical revalidation: 8/12 cases pass at <= 1.5x; the 4 failing cases are exclusively the explicit for-loop body shape (anti-pattern). v2.1.0 originally framed the residual as 'stdlib Collection.map's specialization gap'; that framing has since been further refuted in `stdlib-collection-map-actor-isolation-overhead.md` (2026-05-07) — the per-element cost is the runtime actor-isolation check mandated by SE-0423, conditioned on stdlib's .swiftinterface being built in Swift 5 mode, not a generic-specialization failure.

documents.topic: Phase 1 rollout of the centralized linter harness for swift-primitives. Lands 3 mechanical rules: [DOC-003] ValidateDocumentationComments (swift-format catalog rule, fanout via new sync-swift-format.sh + swift-format-canonical.json) + [API-IMPL-005] one_declaration_per_file (SwiftLint built-in opt-in flip at Tier 1) + [PRIM-FOUND-001] Foundation-family imports (γ-1a workflow migration to Tier 2 SwiftLint custom rule pair). Architecture: 3-tier parent_config: chain — Tier 1 ecosystem-wide canonical at swift-institute/.github/.swiftlint.yml (NEW), Tier 2 swift-primitives org-specific canonical at swift-primitives/.github/.swiftlint.yml (NEW; parent_config Tier 1), Tier 3 per-sub-package overrides (132 files re-targeted from the no-longer-canonical swift-standards/swift-standards URL to the new Tier 2 URL). Apple swift-format has no remote inheritance so canonical .swift-format distributed via fanout (134 files updated by sync-swift-format.sh, mirroring sync-dependabot/sync-swift-settings family pattern). γ-1a migration: deleted lint-foundation-family-import.yml + lint-foundation-family-import-weekly.yml + audit-foundation-import.py + swift-ci.yml caller block + cron-audit-base.yml consumer-list comment reference. Peer CI/CD agent caught the weekly cron deletion (which I'd missed in the original draft); the weekly's cross-org drift-sweep purpose is absorbed by Tier 2 per-PR enforcement. Regex parity verified 24/24 cases (11 attribute permutations + 6 variants + 7 negatives) between deleted Python audit script and new Tier 2 SwiftLint custom rules. Two live user revisions during dispatch: (1) premise correction — swift-standards/swift-standards no longer canonical, re-derived to 3-tier chain at .github org-special repos; (2) source-refactor scope deferred — user explicitly OVERRODE supervisor block 'MUST fix every existing violation' with 'focus on CI; DO NOT exclude to fix; failing CI is expected; we welcome the violations as these point us to where we need to make changes.' Baseline violation counts (the intentional diagnostic surface): [DOC-003] 155 across 33 packages (swift-format linter; not auto-fixable), [API-IMPL-005] 23 across 6 packages (below supervisor codemod-threshold of 30; manual file splits in scope for Phase 2+), [PRIM-FOUND-001] 79 across 6 packages (top: swift-render-primitives 51 + swift-structured-queries-primitives 12 + swift-test-primitives 11; vs brief's expected zero — γ-1a was advisory not gating, so violations accumulated unenforced). Total = 257 violations expected to fail CI on first push wave; intentional. 5-wave push plan: W1 swift-institute/.github (commit 7f53280, 6 files), W2 swift-primitives/.github (commit 8a03db6, 1 file), W2.5 single-canary on swift-carrier or swift-tagged for live HTTP/cache/CDN verification, W3 132 sub-repo commits (uniform 'parent_config re-target + ValidateDocs flip'), W4 workspace-level root files (NOT a git repo; no push), W5 Research repo (this doc + index entry). Phase 2+ source-refactor cleanup is the follow-up dispatch chain.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Author-and-validate dispatch closing Phase 1 of the centralized linter harness rollout. CI infrastructure landed locally + validated; no remote pushes (each wave requires explicit per-action user authorization per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes). Source refactor explicitly deferred to Phase 2+ per live user revision — the 257 violations are the intentional diagnostic surface. Architecture (3-tier SwiftLint URL chain + swift-format fanout) verified mechanically (parent_config chains supported recursively per SwiftLint docs; both .github org-special repos exist as natural canonical hosts; regex parity 24/24 with deleted audit script). Peer CI/CD agent input integrated (V5 catch on missed weekly cron deletion, [API-IMPL-005] Tier 1 placement confirmation, W2.5 canary recommendation, regex parity verification heads-up). All 22 supervisor block entries verified end-to-end; one MUST entry (fix-every-violation-in-same-PR) explicitly overridden by user revision and stamped in HANDOFF Live Revisions section. Push waves W1-W5 await per-action authorization. Phase 2+ dispatches address: 2a [API-IMPL-005] file splits (manual; ~2-4h), 2b [DOC-003] doc-comment fixes (~1.5-2.5h), 2c [PRIM-FOUND-001] Foundation removal (multi-day; domain refactor; some may need primitive-replacement design).

documents.topic: DECISION (v1.1.0): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide foundational re-examination of @safe-attribute usage and absorber-pattern invariant disclosure. Stamped Option B 2026-05-12: embrace SE-0458 @safe per language intent + layer mandatory institute invariant-disclosure requirement on top. Inverts [MEM-SAFE-025b] from forbid-@safe to admit-with-disclosure; introduces [MEM-SAFE-025c] (disclosure required when @safe present). Supersedes wave-4-absorber-pattern-policy-lean-2026-05-12.md. Empirical universe: 125 live @safe sites; three clusters (A direct ~70, B transitive ~30, C pure documentation ~25) all admitted under inverted rule. Structural rationale: @safe is the only language-level mechanism for argument-unsafety suppression and no-propagation guarantee per SE-0458; comments cannot fulfill these roles. Cross-language convention (Rust unsafe + // SAFETY:, Haskell Safe-Haskell + haddocks, PCC proof-witness + proof-obligation) is complementary attribute + comment. Migration ~20-40 invariant-disclosure additions; strict superset closure relative to Wave 4 carve-out (no sites lose @safe). Implementation pass dispatched.
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.0. Implementation pass dispatched.

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.1.0): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide policy research deriving an externally-cited best-practice for pairing (or not pairing) SE-0458 @safe / @unsafe attributes with SE-0302 @unchecked Sendable conformances. v1.1.0 adds a recency-verification pass against swiftlang/swift commits 2025-11 through 2026-05, post-SE-0458 evolution proposals (SE-0459–SE-0530), Apple-package main branches, and the official strict-memory-safety compiler tests; the v1.0.0 conclusion (peers, not partners — do NOT pair them) is CONFIRMED without amendment. Recency-pass additions: (1) refined annotation-position grammar — @unsafe has three syntactic positions (type decl / extension decl / conformance clause), only positions #1 and #2 ever coexist with @unchecked Sendable, position #3 is reserved for non-Sendable protocols; (2) UniqueBox commit-by-commit worked example (2026-01 through 2026-04) — the most recent live demonstration of '@safe type + @unchecked Sendable conformance' with @unsafe appearing nowhere on the type; (3) SE-0458 Future Direction on SerialExecutor verified NOT landed (no post-SE-0458 proposal amends the rule); (4) compiler-test dispositive evidence — test/Unsafe/unsafe_concurrency.swift:11 + test/Unsafe/unsafe-suppression.swift:77-83 codify 'class C: @unchecked Sendable' as the canonical strict-memory-safety pattern. Aggregate empirical count across stdlib + 15 Apple packages on 2026-05-13: several hundred @unchecked Sendable sites, ZERO @unsafe @unchecked Sendable pairs. Deterministic rule added: Sendable carries @unchecked when the compiler can't prove it; @unsafe lives on the unsafe declaration, never on the Sendable conformance.
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.0. Tier 2 ecosystem-wide policy research per [RES-020] (multi-release lifetime, cross-package precedent, informal-to-normative semantic commitment for ~209 @unchecked Sendable sites; reversible if SE-0458 future-work direction lands). Per [RES-021] prior-art survey extended in v1.1.0: SE-0302, SE-0414, SE-0446, SE-0447, SE-0458, SE-0463, SE-0467, SE-0470, SE-0485, SE-0517, SE-0518, SE-0527 + swiftlang/swift stdlib current state (Sendable.swift, Array, Dictionary, Set, Span family, Mutex, Atomic, _StringGuts, UniqueBox, UnsafeSleepStateToken) + apple/swift-collections (main, including BasicContainers preview), apple/swift-foundation, apple/swift-async-algorithms, apple/swift-syntax, apple/swift-nio (2.92.2), apple/swift-atomics, apple/swift-asn1, apple/swift-certificates, apple/swift-crypto, apple/swift-system, apple/swift-argument-parser, apple/swift-log, apple/swift-subprocess (0.2.1), apple/swift-testing, apple/swift-numerics; cross-language: Rust RFC #2585 (non-propagating unsafe precedent). Per [RES-023] empirical-claim verification at write time: every claim about stdlib / third-party convention verified against file:line citations + commit SHAs + dates 2026-05-13. Per [RES-031] independent verification of subagent-reported version-pinned claims: changelog entries (CHANGELOG.md line 194 + line 472) and proposal numbers verified against primary sources (gh api). Per [RES-026] citations: plain Markdown links to canonical SE proposal URLs, GitHub file:line anchors, and commit SHAs throughout References section. Per dispatch's explicit scope constraint, Institute's own ecosystem (swift-primitives/, swift-standards/, swift-foundations/, Audits/, Skills/) intentionally NOT consulted — the load-bearing artifact is the fresh external read.
