Tier 2 cross-package analysis (v1.3.0, status RECOMMENDATION restored after v1.2.0 false-negative deferral) of whether swift-equation-primitives, swift-hash-primitives, and swift-comparison-primitives can be deprecated once SE-0499 lands. The three packages fork Swift.Equatable/Hashable/Comparable specifically because the stdlib protocols required Self: Copyable; SE-0499 (Implemented Swift 6.4) relaxes that. Verification spike at `swift-institute/Experiments/se-0499-stdlib-noncopyable-protocol-conformance/` PASSES against Swift 6.4-dev DEVELOPMENT-SNAPSHOT-2026-03-16-a when the toolchain is selected via TOOLCHAINS=org.swift.64202603161a (env var with bundle identifier). The earlier `xcrun --toolchain '<snapshot-name>' swift build` invocation was a build-config defect — it sets the compiler binary but does NOT redirect SwiftPM's stdlib resolution; the false-negative diagnostics led v1.2.0 to incorrectly defer. The nightly's bundled stdlib `Swift.swiftmodule/.../arm64-apple-macos.swiftinterface` declares `public protocol Equatable : ~Copyable, ~Escapable` (verified by direct grep). Implementation truth: PR #85079 is the umbrella WIP, but the actual landings are split PRs by Ben Cohen — #85746 (Equatable, 2025-12-03), #85747 (Comparable), #85748 (Hashable), plus #85854/#85891 for ~Escapable. Checking only the umbrella PR is insufficient; for SE-0499 the merged-PR truth requires checking the split-PR family (verified against local `/Users/coen/Developer/swiftlang/swift` main, fetched 2026-05-03). Recommendation: Option C-refined — wrap existing fork protocols in `#if swift(<6.4)`, add `#else` branch with `extension Equation { public typealias \`Protocol\` = Swift.Equatable }` etc.; wrap each `+Swift.*` bridge body in `#if swift(<6.4)` (under 6.4 they duplicate stdlib's existing conditional conformances). Per-package surface preserved: hash-primitives keeps Hash.Value (Tagged<Hash, Int>); comparison-primitives keeps the three-way Comparison enum + Comparison.Compare + Comparison.Clamp + Property.View integrations — substantive value-type surface independent of the Comparable fork. equation-primitives sub-decision (full retirement vs transitional shim) deferred to a separate cycle; this cycle only adds the guards. Per-package guard application requires explicit per-package authorization per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes. Supersedes the premise of Equation Primitives Design.md v2.1.0 §11.3 and §17.
documents.statusDetail: v1.3.0. Recommendation: Option C-refined — wrap fork protocols in #if swift(<6.4), add #else typealias to stdlib counterpart, wrap +Swift.* bridges in #if swift(<6.4). Verification spike CONFIRMED on Swift 6.4-dev nightly via TOOLCHAINS=org.swift.64202603161a. Per-package guard application authorized for execution by principal direction (do this proactively, test on swift 6.4) — but each per-package commit + push remains a per-action authorization per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes; the action proceeds inside the workspace and stops at the push boundary unless re-authorized. v1.2.0 was a false-negative deferral due to xcrun --toolchain not redirecting SwiftPM's stdlib resolution; documented in changelog as lesson. Open sub-questions: (a) equation-primitives full retirement vs transitional shim, deferred; (b) Hash.Value survives as one-purpose package or folds into swift-tagged-primitives; (c) Comparison/Compare/Clamp second-consumer check per [MOD-RENT] / [RES-018]; (d) +Identity.Tagged and +Property.View bridge-file per-file audit.

Axis A ([FAM-010]) REINFORCED by 36-system convergence (refinement essentially absent from production-grade libraries) and coherence-theoretic citation (Bottu et al. 2019 arXiv:1907.00844, Racordon 2025 arXiv:2502.20546, ezyang coherence write-up) matching §8's anchor-merging argument; Apple New Codable prototype independently corroborates siblings-not-refinement. v1.1.0 deferred axis B ([FAM-002] semantic) to successor arc with three resolution paths enumerated: (a) preserve [FAM-002] type-commitment scoped via [FAM-003], (b) reframe canonical to author-choice-with-fallback, (c) introduce thin CommonCodable analogue alongside existing canonical. v1.2.0 (2026-05-22) RESOLVES axis B via successor arc canonical-attachment-semantic.md v1.0.0: path (c) sanctioned-but-deferred — codified as new rule [FAM-011]; additive Common.Codable peer alongside existing canonical trio; [FAM-002]'s type-commitment semantic UNCHANGED; new peer's authoring deferred until empirical pull (consumer-driven currency-type need OR Apple New Codable formal pitch). Tipping question settled NO via §4 enumeration in successor doc (protobuf field numbers + XML attribute-vs-element categorically distinct; CBOR/MessagePack/plist absorb at format-shape-leak cost; JSON null-vs-missing-key asymmetrically absorbable) — rules out path (b)'s rich-canonical reframing. §10a Disposition updated from DEFERRED-PENDING-SUCCESSOR-ARC to RESOLVED-VIA-PATH-(c)-DEFERRED. §10 [FAM-010] body unchanged across v1.0.0–v1.2.0. Categorized findings: 5 Tier-1 critical defects (broken cross-references, ghost rules, dangling skill dependencies); 10 Tier-2 substantive items (platform skill split candidate at 1574 lines; persistent heading-spelling defect from 2026-04-15 audit; release-readiness skill gaps; missing changelog/release-notes skill; testing cluster cluster-review candidate); 14 Tier-3 polish items. Cross-cluster patterns identified: (1) ghost references — 5 distinct defects slipping past [AUDIT-028] notation-variant scan; (2) stale-skill verdicts mixed (4 stable bumps, 4 content updates needed); (3) workflow-pipeline cross-reference asymmetry across audit/research/experiment/forums/evolution. Verifies 7 prior findings from 2026-04-15 cluster audit (4 fixed, 1 mitigated, 1 partial, 1 still open). Aggregate effort estimate: ~30-36 hours of skill-maintenance work indicated.

Tier 2 ecosystem-wide extension of `skill-verification-taxonomy-pilot.md` v1.0.0 across the 13 tier-1 infra/process skills (audit, benchmark, ci-cd-workflows, documentation, github-repository, package-export, swift-package, swift-package-build, swift-institute, swift-institute-core, release-readiness, existing-infrastructure, ecosystem-data-structures). Dispatch 1 of 3 in a tiered classification sweep (tier 2 code-shape and tier 3 voice/process are separate dispatches). Pilot's Part 1 reconciliation (three-class reduces/extends four-class; complementary levels; no conflict) carries forward unchanged. Walks 236 requirement IDs across the 13 skills (≈4.1× the pilot's 57); aggregate distribution 135 mechanical / 50 hybrid / 51 semantic = 57.2/21.2/21.6% — materially more mechanical-heavy than the pilot baseline (+11.6 pp on mechanical), confirming the parent-thread hypothesis that infra/process skills concentrate mechanical-rule density. γ-roadmap saturation: 51/135 mechanical rules (37.8%) carry an inline γ-mark; ci-cd-workflows + github-repository carry 78% of all γ matches (the immediate workflow-construction substrate); dominant γ class is γ-2 (YAML/template/metadata lint, 38 matches), then γ-3/γ-3b target-fidelity (8); γ-1a Foundation has 1 (CI-022 ecosystem twin of [PRIM-FOUND-001]); γ-1b license has 2; γ-1c-adjacent API-breakage has 2. Resistant-set: 47/236 (19.9%) — slightly smaller fraction than pilot (22.8%) despite tripled absolute count. Three new resistant patterns surface that pilot's three patterns do not cover: (a) **decision-tree wrappers around mechanical predicates** (existing-infrastructure INFRA-020/-024 et al., 6 cases) — outcome mechanical, but rule's value is at-keyboard routing; (b) **API-gap rules** (github-repository GH-REPO-054, 1 case) — verification blocked by absence of upstream API; (c) **reference-table-as-rule** (package-export PKG-EXPORT-010/-011, 2 cases) — non-normative illustration content wrapped in a requirement ID. All three addressable through additional inline annotations (`**Routing:**`, `**API-Gap:**`, `**Reference:**`); no fourth taxonomy class required. Density outliers: swift-institute-core (0 IDs, manifest-only meta-skill — exclude from rollout-counting denominators); swift-institute (1 ID, degenerate); ecosystem-data-structures (0/1/10, 91% semantic — pure-catalog selection-guidance; tier ordering does not predict mechanical density). release-readiness is the strongest reference-skill candidate for validating the `**Composite:**` annotation (4/6 composite-hybrid). Recommendation: adopt pilot's three remediation clarifications unchanged AND add the three new annotations at the format-change skill update; workflow construction may proceed against the tier-1 mechanical bucket immediately (135 rules, 51 with designed γ checks) without waiting for the format-change update. Honors supervisor block: no SKILL.md edited, no Verification: fields added, no workflow YAML drafted, no Phase-1 triage queue updates applied inline, scope held to 13 tier-1 skills, no remote pushes.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Tier-1 extension classifies 236 requirements at 57/21/22% (mech/hyb/sem); pilot baseline was 46/26/28% — tier-1 +12 pp mechanical confirms infra/process skills' mechanical-rule concentration. 51 γ-roadmap matches across 135 mechanical rules; ci-cd-workflows + github-repository carry 78% of γ matches. Three new resistant patterns added (decision-tree wrappers, API-gap rules, reference-table-as-rule); all addressable through `**Routing:**` / `**API-Gap:**` / `**Reference:**` inline annotations following the pilot's `**Composite:**` / `**External:**` shape. No fourth taxonomy class required. release-readiness recommended as reference-skill sequel to the pilot's code-surface for validating the `**Composite:**` annotation pattern. Workflow construction unblocked against the tier-1 mechanical bucket. Tier 2 (code-shape skills, ~7) and tier 3 (voice/process, ~24) are gated on this dispatch's review.

documents.topic: Tier 2 ecosystem-wide extension of `skill-verification-taxonomy-pilot.md` v1.0.0 + `skill-verification-taxonomy-extension-tier-1.md` v1.0.0 across the 7 tier-2 code-shape skills (conversions, index, memory-arithmetic, memory-safety, modularization, platform, swift-package-heritage). Dispatch 2 of 3 in the tiered classification sweep; tier-3 voice/process is the final dispatch gated on this one's review. Pilot's Part 1 reconciliation + tier-1's six-annotation set carry forward unchanged. Walks 208 requirement IDs across the 7 skills (193 unique once 15 [IDX-*] rules shared between conversions and index are deduplicated to canonical home); aggregate distribution 107 mechanical / 58 hybrid / 43 semantic = 51.4/27.9/20.7% (49.7/29.0/21.2% on the unique-rule view). Tier-2 mechanical density sits between pilot (45.6%) and tier-1 (57.2%) as the brief predicted; **hybrid share peaks at tier-2 (27.9%)** — code-shape rules invite "AST prefilter + intent-of-use judgment" structure more than infra/process rules do. γ-roadmap saturation: 2/107 mechanical rules (1.9%) carry γ-marks (PATTERN-005, PATTERN-006 — Package.swift settings linting); tier-2 is functionally γ-empty as the brief predicted because v1.2.0's γ family targets workflow infrastructure, not SwiftPM target schemas / namespace structure / ownership shape. Latent γ-2 candidate clusters surfaced for future extension: 7 modularization Package.swift schema rules ([MOD-001/005/006/007/012/015/024]), 17 index type-shape rules, 8 memory-arithmetic operator-overload rules — each a distinct deterministic-scan family against a closed schema. Resistant-set: 63/208 raw (30.3%); 22.1% after deducting Reference-shape sub-pattern (ii) definitional-anchor cases (which classify cleanly as mechanical). **No seventh resistant pattern surfaces** — six-pattern soft ceiling holds. Three findings: (1) Reference-shape sub-split — 28 cases divide into pure-illustration (11 — drop-candidate per pilot recommendation) and definitional-anchor (17 — retention-with-annotation, mechanical predicate exists but rule's role is API-surface documentation); optional sub-shape disambiguator `**Reference (illustration):**` / `**Reference (anchor):**`. (2) Routing-pattern generalization — tier-1 framed Routing as wrapper around mechanical predicates; tier-2 surfaces Routing on semantic-classed rules too (CONV-016/015, MOD-008/RENT, PLAT-ARCH-002, HERITAGE-005/006); annotation generalizes to "rule's value is decision-tree shape" regardless of leaf-class. (3) Cross-skill ID overlap — conversions and index share 15 [IDX-*] requirement IDs declared with full bodies in both SKILL.md files; 2/15 classify differently across the two skills (IDX-006c, IDX-010 — index→mechanical, conversions→hybrid); surfaced as implicit Composite signal (recommended skill-cleanup: collapse declarations to canonical home). Density outliers: index 77.3% mech (typed-API skill confirmed), swift-package-heritage 28.6% mech (origin/governance semantic-heavy confirmed), platform 36.7% hybrid (highest hybrid share across pilot+tier-1+tier-2 — structural rules combine grep-prefilter with tier/domain judgment). The conversions Reference-shape cluster (14 definitional-anchor cases) was the most material UNanticipated finding. Recommendation: adopt pilot+tier-1's six annotations unchanged; optionally adopt the Reference sub-shape disambiguator; no seventh class needed. Tier-2's contribution to workflow-construction substrate is not immediate (only 2 γ matches) — the rules would feed NEW γ-classes (SwiftPM target-shape audit, Swift-AST type-shape audit) not yet in v1.2.0 §3.4. Tier-2's value is taxonomy validation, not workflow input. Workflow construction continues against tier-1 bucket. Honors supervisor block: no SKILL.md edited, no Verification: fields added, no workflow YAML drafted, no Phase-1 triage queue updates applied inline, scope held to 7 tier-2 skills, no remote pushes, cross-session contamination (wasm-ci doc, untracked Reflections, Reflections/_index.json mods, _index.json wasm-ci entry) deliberately not staged.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Tier-2 extension classifies 208 requirements (193 unique after IDX-* dedup) at 51.4/27.9/20.7% mechanical/hybrid/semantic — between pilot and tier-1 on mech density as predicted; hybrid peaks at tier-2 (code-shape rules invite prefilter+judgment structure). Only 2 γ-roadmap matches (1.9%) — tier-2 functionally γ-empty as predicted; v1.2.0's γ family targets workflow infrastructure, not code-shape rules. Three findings surfaced: Reference-shape sub-split (illustration vs definitional-anchor), Routing-pattern generalization to semantic rules, conversions↔index 15-rule overlap as implicit Composite signal. No seventh resistant pattern; six-annotation soft ceiling holds. Density calibration: index 77% mech (confirmed), swift-package-heritage 29% mech (confirmed semantic-heavy), platform 37% hybrid (highest across pilot+tier-1+tier-2). Latent γ-class candidates surfaced for future v1.2.0 extension: SwiftPM target-shape audit (modularization), Swift-AST type-shape audit (index, memory-arithmetic). Workflow construction continues against tier-1 bucket; tier-2's value is taxonomy validation. Tier-3 voice/process (~24 skills) is the final dispatch in the sweep, gated on this one's review.

documents.topic: Tier 3 (FINAL) ecosystem-wide extension of `skill-verification-taxonomy-pilot.md` v1.0.0 + tier-1 + tier-2 across the 24 voice/process skills (collaborative-discussion, corpus-meta-analysis, document-markup, experiment-process, handoff, implementation, issue-investigation, quick-commit-and-push-all, readme, reflect-session, reflections-processing, research-process, skill-lifecycle, supervise, swift-evolution, swift-forums-review, swift-pull-request, testing, testing-institute, testing-swiftlang, dutch-law, legal-encoding, legal-testing, rule-law-core). Dispatch 3 of 3 — closes the tiered classification sweep. Pre-flight escalation: 9 additional engagement+ingest skills (`swift-institute/Engagement/Skills/`) accessible via .claude/skills symlinks but explicitly out of brief scope (private sibling repo per `feedback_skills_follow_institute_convention.md`); flagged in §28.3 as scope-expansion candidate, NOT classified per supervisor MUST clause. Walks 544 requirement IDs across 24 skills (mean 22.7/skill); aggregate 263 mechanical / 146 hybrid / 135 semantic = 48.3/26.8/24.8%. **Tier-3 mechanical density (48%) is comparable to tier-2 (51%), NOT "dominantly semantic" as the brief expected** — operational sub-clusters (testing-* 80–100%, dutch-law/legal-encoding 55–86%, document-markup 76%, swift-pull-request 82%) maintain a robust mechanical floor offsetting the semantic-heavy supervise (26%) and research-process (16%) clusters. Semantic share rebounds modestly (+4.1 pp vs tier-2). γ-roadmap saturation: 28/263 mechanical rules (10.6%) carry γ-marks (26 γ-2 in readme/ci-automation cluster + 1 γ-1b + 1 γ-1c in placeholder + 1 γ-2 in corpus-meta-analysis META-021); concentrated almost entirely in readme. Most are aspirational (carry **API-Gap** annotation — workflows specified-pending). Resistant set: ~190/544 (35%) raw — highest of any tier — driven by Routing (~50 cases) and Reference (anchor) (~50 cases) cluster expansion at scale. After deducting Reference (anchor) cases that classify cleanly, genuinely-resistant fraction drops to ~25%. **No seventh resistant pattern surfaces** — six-pattern soft ceiling holds across all four dispatches. **Ecosystem-wide closure: 47 skills, 1045 requirements, 531 mechanical / 269 hybrid / 245 semantic = 50.8/25.7/23.4%** — roughly half mechanical, quarter hybrid, quarter semantic. Mechanical-rule construction queue (§28.2) ranks all 47 skills by mechanical count: top 10 carry 220 mechanical rules (41% of total). Active γ cluster (≈80 rules with designed checks) feeds immediate workflow-construction substrate; remaining ≈451 mechanical rules form latent reservoir awaiting γ-class extension across 5 candidate families (skill-shape audit, test-shape audit, legal-source-fetch, process-discipline, markup-API). Density outliers triggered: testing-institute & quick-commit-and-push-all 100% mech; dutch-law universal **External:** annotation (17/17 rules); supervise 26% mech as judgment-bearing skill; implementation 0% (4 hub axioms only, sibling-file corollary rules out of scope per brief); readme 83-ID outlier driven by hub+sibling structure with 11 reserved-ID slots. skill-lifecycle has 2 template-literal placeholder `### [` headings inside SKILL-CREATE-005's example block, not classifiable rules — 30 real rules walked. Recommendation: adopt pilot+tier-1+tier-2's six annotations unchanged; optionally adopt Reference sub-shape disambiguator; no seventh class needed. Workflow construction phase begins (separate dispatch) with active γ cluster as immediate substrate, latent reservoir as second-priority requiring v1.2.0 §3.4 γ-class extension. Tier-3 closes the classification sweep; no further classification dispatches scheduled. The 9 engagement+ingest skills surfaced as scope-expansion candidate for supervisor consideration but NOT required for sweep closure. Honors supervisor block: no SKILL.md edited, no Verification: fields added, no workflow YAML drafted, no Phase-1 triage queue updates applied inline, scope held to 24 tier-3 skills, no remote pushes, cross-session contamination preserved untouched.

Ecosystem-wide closure: **47 skills, 1045 requirements, 531m/269h/245s = 50.8/25.7/23.4% — roughly half mechanical, quarter each of hybrid and semantic.** γ-saturation 10.6% in tier-3 (concentrated in readme/ci-automation aspirational marks); ecosystem-wide 80/531 mechanical rules (15%) carry active γ matches. documents.topic: Tier 2 ecosystem-wide pilot validating a proposed three-class skill-requirement verification taxonomy (mechanical / hybrid / semantic) against the prior four-class CI-graduation scheme in `centralized-swift-ci-and-spine-gate.md` v1.2.0 §3.4.10 (deterministic / pilot-classified / fidelity-classified / judgment-based). Reconciliation: the three-class **reduces** the four-class on the hybrid axis (collapses pilot-classified ∪ fidelity-classified) and **extends** it across all skill requirements, including those without active CI rollout — the schemes operate at complementary levels (skill-requirement design-time vs CI-check operational lifecycle), no irreconcilable conflict. Three pilot subjects walked: code-surface (27 IDs; 15 mechanical / 6 hybrid / 6 semantic), primitives (7 IDs; 4 / 1 / 2 — includes the canonical mechanical case [PRIM-FOUND-001]), blog-process (23 IDs; 7 / 8 / 8). Aggregate distribution 26/15/16 (≈46/26/28%) across 57 walked requirements. Resistant set 13/57 (22.8%) in three classes: composite multi-mechanism rules (5; HIGH materiality), workflow-vs-outcome rules (5; MEDIUM), external-knowledge-fetch rules (3; LOW). Recommendation: adopt three-class taxonomy with three skill-format clarifications (composite-rule splitting/annotation, workflow-vs-outcome encoding, external-knowledge cost annotation); rewrite ONE reference skill per [SKILL-LIFE-026] before ecosystem-wide rollout; defer GitHub Actions workflow YAML drafting until reference skill stabilizes. Honors supervisor block: no skill files edited, no workflow YAML drafted, scope held to 3 pilots, no token-consuming sweep initiated.

documents.topic: Snapshot naming patterns: cross-ecosystem analysis — named vs unnamed snapshots, inline vs file-backed storage, API design across 8 frameworks (swift-snapshot-testing, Jest, Vitest, Insta, cargo-insta, Verify, ApprovalTests.NET, snapshot_testing Ruby). Key finding: no framework supporting both inline and file-backed uses auto-numbering for inline snapshots.

Snapshot testing and inline snapshot testing literature study (Tier 3, v1.1) — SLR (45 sources: Barr oracle taxonomy, Rothermel regression theory, Feathers characterization tests, Myers diff). 14 ecosystems surveyed (Rust insta, OCaml ppx_expect, JS Jest/Vitest, Python inline-snapshot, JVM Selfie, Swift PointFree). Formal semantics (oracle, serializer canonicity, source rewrite, typing rules, soundness theorem). Cognitive Dimensions analysis (inline > file-based on 4 dimensions). Swift 6.2+ enablers (pre-built SwiftSyntax, ST-0009 Attachments, SE-0451 raw identifiers). Infrastructure mapping: optics-primitives (redaction composition algebra), tree-primitives (structural diff via Tree.Keyed), json/xml (canonical serialization), witnesses/dependencies (scoped configuration). 5 priority improvements with revised effort estimates.

Strategy for ecosystem-wide GitHub repository social preview cards across the swift-institute 130+ repo surface (Primitives L1 / Standards L2 / Foundations L3 / Components L4 / Institute org-of-orgs). Operationalizes the 'social preview images' future-surface enumerated in github-metadata-harmonization.md §13. Critical empirical [RES-023] finding: GitHub exposes NO public token-authenticated API for SETTING a repository's social preview image — confirmed by GitHub staff on Community Discussion #172072 (2025-09-04). REST PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo} accepts no preview field; GraphQL Repository.openGraphImageUrl is query-only with no mutation; gh repo edit has no flag; README og:image is not honored as fallback for github.com URLs. The undocumented /upload/repository-images/ endpoint is session-cookie-authenticated (user_session — password-equivalent, not PAT-scoped), exploited by AnswerDotAI/gh-social-preview (Playwright) and drogers0/gh-image (browser cookie store) — both incompatible with feedback_no_gh_cli_admin_scope.md auth posture. Hard spec verified: 1280×640 px, PNG/JPG/GIF, <1 MB, 40 pt safe-area border (synthesized to 1200×540 centered safe-area to also survive 1.91:1 LinkedIn/FB center-crop), 48 px minimum effective font for thumbnail legibility. Prior art survey [RES-021] of 19 OSS repos: 4/19 (21%) ship custom cards (vapor/vapor + vapor/fluent — ecosystem template with glyph+wordmark slot; vercel/next.js — monogram + product tagline; supabase/supabase — wordmark + tagline + screenshot); 15/19 ship GitHub auto-generated defaults including ALL 15 surveyed Swift repos (apple/swift, swiftlang/swift-syntax, apple/swift-collections, apple/swift-numerics, apple/swift-foundation, apple/swift-nio, apple/swift-system, pointfreeco/swift-composable-architecture, pointfreeco/swift-dependencies, ChartsOrg/Charts, realm/SwiftLint, Quick/Quick) and rust-lang/rust, tokio-rs/tokio, stripe/stripe-node — strong precedent that custom cards are not table-stakes for research-grade infrastructure OSS. Four options analyzed: A=cookie-automation (REJECT — auth-posture fail), B=generation-in-CI + manual one-time per-repo upload (RECOMMENDED — matches Vapor pattern; chassis from existing tier avatars at swift-institute.org/avatars/; reusable workflow generate-social-preview.yml alongside sync-metadata.yml; commits .github/social-preview.png; nightly cron + lint-social-preview-staleness.yml drift detector), C=generation-only no-deployment (VIABLE fallback — degenerate form of B), D=no-customization Apple posture (DEFENSIBLE status quo — 15/15 Swift surveyed). Card composition: tier glyph (4-bar pyramid from existing avatar SVG, gradient bar in tier-correct position) left third + repo display name in heavy sans ≥72 px right two-thirds + 4 px tier-gradient bottom rule. Five open questions surfaced for implementing dispatch (background variant, display-name source, multi-target packages, cohort-tier mapping, institute-internal-repo coverage). Implementation gates per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes.md: workflow authoring, chassis SVG, per-repo backfill all require explicit per-action authorization.
documents.statusDetail: v2.0.0 (2026-05-07): Promoted RECOMMENDATION → DECISION. Implementation pivoted from v1.0.0's Option B (CI generation + manual upload + committed PNG) to a hybrid path: Option A's mechanism (Playwright + session cookie for the undocumented /upload/repository-images/ endpoint) but RUN LOCALLY on a maintainer's laptop, never in CI — sidesteps the auth-posture rejection by keeping the password-equivalent cookie in the same security envelope as opening Settings → Social preview manually. PNG is NOT committed to target repos (treated as derived state). Vendored AnswerDotAI/gh-social-preview with --image flag added at swift-institute/Scripts/social-preview-uploader/. Deployed across 9 orgs (~80+ public repos) with bronze/PRIMITIVES, silver/STANDARDS, gold/FOUNDATIONS branding. Operational state in Skills/social-preview/SKILL.md ([SOC-001..010]).

documents.topic: Reference note documenting the SPM `swift build --build-tests` parallelism artifact class — `error: no such module '<X>'` / `error: missing required module '<X>'` diagnostics that do NOT represent structural Package.swift gaps and evaporate under clean `.build` + `-j 1`. Six concrete observations across five upstream packages (Standard_Library_Extensions, Witness_Primitives, System_Primitives, Tagged_Primitives, Terminal_Input_Primitives + Index_Primitives_Core + Kernel_Primitives_Core) collected during the D5 sweep + D3+D6 re-derivation cycles 2026-04-24 → 2026-04-25. documents.topic: SPM nested package publication feasibility (Tier 2) — empirical validation (12 test configurations), prior art survey (10 proposals/pitches, 8 major packages), Swift Forums confirmation. Nested Package.swift rejected (hard SPM limitation). Current three-repo layered model confirmed as only viable architecture. Git subtree evaluation deferred.

Architectural role of swift-*-standard packages after spec implementations split into standards-body orgs (Tier 2, v2.0) — domain shape taxonomy (primitive-first vs standard-first, single-spec vs multi-spec), two purposes (convergence + stability), three-layer model (body orgs → swift-standards → swift-foundations), stable-vs-volatile separation. Recommends keeping all 17 packages in swift-standards/. Withdraws v1.0 "eliminate thin re-exports" recommendation.

Refutes the 'stdlib Collection.map specialization gap' framing carried in result-builder-performance-optimization.md v2.1.0's Residual section. Empirical: V8 stdlib .map measures ~4318 ns/iter at N=100 vs V14 same-module @inlinable map at ~197 ns/iter when called from @MainActor top-level code in a Swift 6 SwiftPM target (~22x). When the same call is routed through a `nonisolated` function the gap vanishes (V8n ~202 ns, V14n ~202 ns). Single-file `swiftc -O` does not reproduce the gap (1.07x). SIL inspection: V8's specialized closure body emits per-element `swift_task_isCurrentExecutor` + `swift_task_reportUnexpectedExecutor`; V14's does not. Both have the closure literal fully specialized (`smul_with_overflow_Int64` for `$0 * 2`). Generic specialization is not failing. Mechanism: SE-0423 (Implemented Swift 6.0) mandates a runtime actor-isolation check at each invocation when an isolated synchronous closure is passed to a non-strict-concurrency module's API; stdlib's binary .swiftinterface is built with `-swift-version 5`, causing `isConcurrencyChecked()` to return false at the import boundary. Confirmed by harlanhaskins's closing comment on PR #82795 (closed without merge 2025-07-24). Recommendation (C): accept and document. Reject (A) report upstream — already known and tracked. Reject (B) ship `Collection.fastMap` workaround helpers — they would be a transition-period synonym for stdlib map that becomes a deprecation cost when stdlib's .swiftinterface migrates. Structural workaround: hoist hot HOF call sites out of @MainActor scope into `nonisolated` functions or methods. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide, DECISION.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Empirically refutes the v2.1.0 specialization-gap framing on three configurations of the experiment plus SIL inspection. Cites SE-0423 and PR #82795 as primary sources. Per-element overhead (44.7 ns) measured. No upstream report; no workaround API; documentation of the gotcha and the structural fix.

Recommends ~20 concrete linear operators + existing type-erased concurrent operators.

Stream isolation propagation analysis (Tier 2) — every operator breaks isolation (100% breakage). Root causes: `@Sendable` `_next`, sync closures cannot be nonsending, actor state, unstructured Tasks. Option D: accept stream as concurrency boundary.

DECISION (v1.0.2, A1 implementation landed + A2 measurement gate PASSED): Tier 2 comparative analysis + implemented architecture for closing swift-json's 37% parse+decode gap to Foundation's JSONDecoder on schema-known partial-shape workloads. Predecessor: streaming-json-deserialize-status-quo-and-prior-art.md v1.0.0. v1.0.0 enumerated four candidate architectures; recommended Option B (event-emitting Span parser γ). v1.0.1 recorded A0 disposition: Premise 1 GREEN (Token.Kind storage incl .unknown(UInt8)), Premise 3 GREEN (withContiguousStorageIfAvailable), Premise 2 GREEN compile+correctness + RED §4.3 timing (Bar/Today=4.48× mock); implementation-side JSON.assemble short-circuit locked in as REQUIRED A1 constraint. v1.0.2 records A1 disposition: Wave 0 + Wave 1 landed across 6 commits in swift-rfc-8259 + swift-json (Span.EventStream cursor + JSON.Assemble with short-circuit + Serializable.deserialize(events:) protocol + foundational conformer event-grain methods + bench codable-lookup-event-grain mode + oracle migration + 55 new tests; 212 rfc-8259 tests + 47 swift-json tests passing). A2 measurement gate PASSED both axes DECISIVELY on canonical 86 MB Swift stdlib symbol-graph: axis (a) event-grain / Foundation = 0.357× (swift-json 2.80× FASTER than Foundation, vs ≤1.10× projected target — gap substantially reversed); axis (b) default-fallback 5.3% noise-floor delta vs status-quo init(jsonBytes:) (short-circuit working as designed). Wave 1 oracle migration: 0.149s release wall-clock = 6.4× release-to-release speedup; 136 refinement pairs byte-identical. Two acceptable deviations: peekStructural() helper added; consumeAsParseValue is public not SPI (SPI+@inlinable cross-module empirically REJECTED by Swift 6.3.2 compiler, captured in doc + commit f4f3cb2). §10 NEW captures full A1 disposition + three caveats: (a) simdjson framing intentionally DROPPED — theoretical not measured; (b) 0.357× is workload-specific to 67%-skip-ratio schema, low-skip workloads see narrower wedge or none; (c) Wave 2 (Lint.Manifest + Manifest.Load fanout) + Wave 3 (test infra) remain deferred per minimal-scope decision and [BENCH-010] / [RES-018]. Status upgraded RECOMMENDATION → DECISION at v1.0.2. Implementations field in frontmatter cites all 7 commits. Original v1.0.0 / v1.0.1 analysis preserved per [RES-008]; §9 / §10 record the A0 / A1 dispositions respectively.

RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 2 descriptive characterization of how swift-json today performs the parse → typed-decode path (full-tree-then-extract) and the architectural shapes the JSON-decoding field uses for the same task. Motivated by parse-performance.md v1.2.1's measured 37% structural gap between swift-json's JSON.Serializable.init(jsonBytes:) and Foundation's JSONDecoder on the schema-known partial-shape decode path (canonical 86 MB Swift symbol-graph workload, Symbol schema reading 3 of ~9 keys per object). Status-quo characterization: swift-json's call graph (JSON.Serializable.init(jsonBytes:) → JSON.parse → RFC_8259.Decode → RFC_8259.Span.Parser → RFC_8259.Value → JSON wrapper → deserialize) fully materializes every byte before any consumer code runs; ~⅔ of work on canonical workload is immediately discarded. RFC_8259.Token enum exists at the public surface but is bypassed by the Phase A1 Span parser (toolchain-bug-driven). Tier 4 Span parser substrate is ~Copyable & ~Escapable over Span<UInt8> with lifetime annotations; mirrors Binary.Bytes.Input.View. The contracts a redesign must preserve: typed throws (JSON.Error), Foundation-freedom (production Sources/), ~Copyable cursor discipline, strict memory safety, public API stability, the 14× dynamic-access lookup advantage, JSON.Serializable as Codable alternative per codable-untyped-throws-disposition.md. Five-system prior-art survey (simdjson DOM vs ondemand, serde_json Visitor + IgnoredAny, jsoniter Iterator, Apple JSONDecoder + JSONScanner/JSONMap, System.Text.Json Utf8JsonReader, Newtonsoft JsonTextReader) with all claims verified against primary sources by parallel subagents per [RES-020]/[RES-021]. Pattern taxonomy: (α) full-tree DOM materialise-then-walk (status quo); (β) pre-built structural index + lazy decode (modern Foundation JSONDecoder since 2023 rewrite — exact precedent; simdjson ondemand); (γ) pull-driven event stream into target (serde_json, jsoniter, Utf8JsonReader, JsonTextReader). The field has matured past α for schema-known decode at scale; the substrate for either β or γ already exists in swift-rfc-8259 and swift-parser-primitives. Five academic citations: Langdale & Lemire 2019 (simdjson), Mison (Li et al. 2017), Sparser (Palkar et al. 2018), JSONSki (Jiang & Zhao ASPLOS 2022), JSR-173 (StAX). Outcome is descriptive — no prescription; Phase 2 produces the prescription via a separate /research-process invocation. Five loose ends classified per [RES-027] (2 premises, 3 directions).

RFC_8259.Span.EventStream + RFC_8259.Span.Assemble are generic streaming-parser machinery (pull-driven event cursor with depth tracking + structural skip; event-to-tree assembler), not RFC-8259-specific spec content. Under a stricter [ARCH-LAYER-*] reading, neither should live at L2 in swift-rfc-8259; the right home is a new (or existing) L1 primitives package with swift-rfc-8259 as a thin RFC-8259 specialisation. Reclassified 2026-05-14 under the [RES-018] amendment (v1.1.0) as case (c) — layer-agnostic primitive surfacing inside an L2 package, where pull-down to L1 is the architecturally mandated default. swift-rfc-8259 IS the first consumer; the second-consumer hurdle is retired. Remaining gates are technical: (a) performance-residual disposition (~12 ms residual above pre-T-1 baseline post-A+B; tracked in parse-performance.md v1.3.0); (b) Phase 2 comparative shape-analysis. Surfaced 2026-05-14 by principal mid-session as addendum to the placement audit's framing.
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.0 reclassified as case (c) layer-agnostic primitive surfacing in L2; v1.0.0 second-consumer-hurdle framing retired. Remaining gates technical (performance-residual disposition + Phase 2 shape-analysis).

Decision rubric for sub-product splits in swift-primitives. Codifies priority order across three competing signals — upstream-pruning (Gate 1, dominant during pre-1.0 dev), structural axis [MOD-003] (Gate 2), capability rent [MOD-RENT] consumer criterion (Gate 3, PUBLIC-RELEASE only) — under [MOD-DOMAIN] coherence as Gate 0 hard precondition. Resolves the recurring misframing where sibling-variant split candidates were evaluated on downstream consumer count (the Gate 3 signal) in isolation, leading to proposals that passed conceptual cleanliness but failed upstream effect. Worked failure examples: Buffer Linear, Memory Pool, Async Channel — each passes Gate 0/2 but fails Gate 1 (no upstream-tree compression on extraction), so each stays as sibling target. Counter-example: LEB128 / algebra-primitives split — passes Gate 1 (tier compression from 8 to 3, freeing numeric-primitives consumer). Operationalizes upstream-pruning measurement by reference to the [MOD-025] uniquely-providing-dep audit. Lifecycle gating: consumer-criterion sub-rule of [MOD-RENT] is deferred from development to PUBLIC-RELEASE/TAG time per feedback_correctness_sole_driver_during_development. Three anti-patterns explicitly rejected: downstream-consumer-count framing as primary signal; conceptual-purity defense without upstream effect; file-count optimization mistaken for upstream-pruning. Decision procedure given as operational flow (Gate 0 → 1 → 2 → lifecycle check → optional Gate 3 at release).

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide design proposal surfaced by HANDOFF Wave 3 thread #8. swift-linter currently has no per-finding disable mechanism; the rule message references SwiftLint's `// swiftlint:disable:next` which the swift-linter engine does not recognize. Empirical anchor: 2 unchecked_call_site findings on Ownership.Slot.Move.swift:51,59 are genuine institute escape hatches that the rule cannot exempt structurally. Surfaces four options: A line-comment protocol (mirror SwiftLint syntax), B custom attribute, C config-file entries, D hybrid (A primary + C for rule-wide disables — provisional lean). Includes engine-implementation sketch (~150 LOC), migration plan for 40 existing swiftlint:disable sites, meta-audit rule for orphan suppressions. Decision pending; would land as engine-level addition + rule message text updates.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Tier 2 design-proposal-first per HANDOFF Wave 3 thread #8 explicit constraint ('Design proposal first (research-process); engine implementation second'). Four options enumerated with pros/cons + comparison matrix on eight criteria. Provisional lean is Option D (hybrid). Decision pending user/principal deliberation.

documents.topic: Tier-2 RECOMMENDATION: the L1/L2/L3 layering for the SwiftPM package domain. Surfaced by the swift-package-graph + swift-impact arc which audited the domain ownership of SwiftPM types. Establishes: (1) swift-package-primitives stays L1-generic (Package.Name/Product.Name/Target.Name typed identifiers only); (2) new L2 swift-spm-standard at swift-standards/ owns the SwiftPM PackageDescription DSL as Swift types — Package.Manifest, Package.Dependency (with .path/.url(Requirement)/.registry), Package.Requirement (.from/.upToNextMajor/.upToNextMinor/.range/.exact/.branch/.revision), Package.Identity (SE-0292), Manifest.Product (+ Kind/LibraryType), Manifest.Target (+ Kind), TargetDependency, SupportedPlatform, Platform — plus JSON Codable conformances matching `swift package dump-package` wire format; (3) L3 swift-package-manager foundation deferred per [RES-018] (only 1 direct consumer today; trigger is a mutation use case OR ≥2 direct consumers). Migration plan: Phase 0a author swift-spm-standard, Phase 0b refactor swift-package-primitives (remove migrated Package.Dependency types), Phase 0c update ecosystem consumers; Phase 1+2 swift-package-graph and swift-impact respectively. Pre-1.0 breaking changes acceptable per memory feedback_no_inter_launch_soak.md. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

SwiftPM build plugins for `-Xfrontend` flags (Tier 2) — can build plugins inject `-Xfrontend -enable-experimental-feature` flags without `.unsafeFlags()`? Currently `.unsafeFlags()` disables SPM dependency resolution for downstream consumers, blocking adoption of packages using experimental features (Lifetimes, RawLayout).

Investigation of architectural options for replacing regex-based SwiftLint custom rules with AST-based / semantic-shape-based enforcement in the swift-primitives ecosystem. Triggered by the R1–R4 cardinal/ordinal/vector enforcement-cohort cleanup (waves 1+2a+2b+2a-rerun, 50 sites across 21 packages) which surfaced 5 demonstrated regex-evasion classes (paren-wrap caught by companion regex 7622a8b, typename-swap caught by broadened R4 regex 7622a8b, cast-outside + algebraic-flip caught by companion regex c252a39, operand-reorder NOT catchable by clean regex — supervisor diff-review is the current gate, comments-as-code workaround-only via Unicode minus discipline) plus 2 deferred AST-shaped rule classes from cardinal-ordinal-vector-enforcement-design.md (R5 __unchecked: at call-sites only requiring call-vs-decl AST distinction, R0 positive enforcement of [CONV-016] preference hierarchy requiring conversion-tier classification on AST) plus 2 further deferred rules from rollout-phase-1-results.md v1.1.0 ([API-IMPL-005] one_declaration_per_file with per-path scoping for the [TEST-005] @Suite-category fixture pattern — SwiftLint built-in does not accept per-rule path scoping, only severity:; [TEST-025] test_support_module_in_tests with file-context awareness). Eight architectural options surveyed (A SwiftLint native AST custom rules via @SwiftSyntaxRule + Bazel build, B swift-format custom rules eliminated by closed catalog, C SwiftSyntax standalone CLI tool, D SwiftPM build-tool plugin, E SwiftPM command plugin eliminated as primary, F symbol-graph harvesting + post-processing extending the Phase-1-executed pipeline at swift-primitives/Scripts/{extract,distill,analyze}-symbol-graphs.*, G editor LSP eliminated as initial scope, H compiler-plugin macro eliminated as primary). Q1–Q6 sub-questions answered per dispatch brief: evasion-class coverage matrix, deferred-rule unblocking matrix, integration with the existing Tier 1/2/3 parent_config: SwiftLint chain, dev cost / payback per [RES-022] theoretical grounding (regex/AST gap as operational-semantics fact: regex matches surface text, AST matches grammar; 5 evasion classes correspond to lossy projections from grammar to text), per [RES-025] empirical validation via Cognitive Dimensions Framework on the recommended option (visibility/consistency/role-expressiveness all High; viscosity/error-proneness Low; abstraction Appropriate). Prior-art survey [RES-021] across Swift ecosystem (SwiftLint built-in rules, swift-format closed catalog, jpsim's swiftlint-bazel-example, swift-macro-toolkit, Periphery, SwiftFormat-nicklockwood, usami-k SwiftLintPlugin, stackotter swift-lint-plugin) and parallel ecosystems (Rust clippy + dylint cautionary, Go analysis package + golangci-lint as closest 'good outcome' precedent, ESLint user-authored AST plugins, Roslyn NuGet-distributed analyzers, Sorbet, Pylint/mypy/ruff cautionary on fragmentation). Recommendation: Option C (SwiftSyntax-based standalone CLI as new Swift package per developer-tool-package-architecture.md DECISION shape — standalone in Developer/, future-promotable to swift-tools/ superrepo) AUGMENTED by Option F (extend existing symbol-graph pipeline for disjoint signature-shape rule cluster). Six-phase phasing: Phase 1 scaffolding + R5 first rule (1 week), Phase 2 migrate R1–R4 evasion cluster including operand-reorder gain (2 weeks), Phase 3 R0 positive enforcement (3–4 weeks), Phase 4 [API-IMPL-005] per-path + [TEST-025] file-context (1 week), Phase 5 ~30 complex multi-decl rules from mechanical-rule-tool-classification-swift-primitives.md (3–6 months incremental), Phase 6 parallel symbol-graph extension per ai-context-reduction-via-type-system-tooling.md Phase 2 (2–4 weeks). Top risks: distribution friction (mitigated by Periphery/SwiftFormat precedent), rule-predicate precision especially R0 (mitigated by dual-fire warning window), SwiftSyntax version drift (institute already pins via macro packages), scope creep into closed-form regex rules (process discipline). Refines the prior 'no 5th bucket' finding from mechanical-rule-tool-classification-swift-primitives.md v1.0.0: not contradicted as classification-time reasoning, but extended with empirical sub-classification within swiftlint-custom into Tier 5a (regex-feasible: stay in SwiftLint regex — Foundation imports, L1 platform conditionals, filename conventions) and Tier 5b (regex-fragile, AST-feasible: move to new linter — R0–R5, [API-IMPL-005] per-path, [TEST-025] file-context, ~30 complex multi-decl rules).

Recommendation Option C (SwiftSyntax standalone CLI augmented by Option F symbol-graph extension) gated by 6-phase phasing with explicit out-of-scope items (no SwiftLint replacement, no swift-format migration, no editor/LSP integration in initial scope, no compiler-plugin/macro replacement, rule-name finalization deferred to implementation dispatch, no generalization beyond swift-primitives in v1.0.0). documents.topic: System vs Kernel.System namespace boundary (Tier 2) — prior art survey (BSD sysctl CTL_HW/CTL_KERN, Linux sysfs /sys/devices/system/ vs /sys/kernel/, Go, Rust, Apple swift-system). Finding: hardware facts (CPU count, RAM, page size) universally separated from kernel operations; no precedent puts them under "kernel". Recommends Option D: System as complete typed hardware namespace (System.Processor.Count, System.Memory.Capacity, System.Page.Size), Kernel.System retains only OS-operational queries (Name, Path.Length, sleep).

How to resolve Kernel.Path/String identity when both wrap Memory.Contiguous\<Char\> (Tier 2) — 7 options (A–G). Decision: Option G — concrete types as RawValue, domain as Tag. Kind × Domain = two orthogonal axes. Validated by tagged-two-level-lifetime (6/6 CONFIRMED)
Can Tagged<Element, Cardinal> prove structural Sendable when Element: Sendable? (Tier 1) — could remove @unchecked from phantom-typed containers

Task-local stack unification (Tier 2, v3.0) — structural unification of L1 Dependency.Scope and L3 Witness.Context @TaskLocal stacks. Triggered by architectural dissatisfaction with closure-chain bridging mechanism during Phase 3 L1 Key Bridge implementation.

TCA26 (ComposableArchitecture 2.0) isolation patterns investigation: 10 patterns analyzed, 5 recommendations for swift-io (non-Sendable closures for confined work, shared Runtime protocol, two-phase drain, explicit nonsending annotations, preserve ~Copyable/~Escapable). Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

TCA26 isolation patterns applied to swift-async-primitives (L1) and swift-async (L3). Per-pattern analysis: F2 (non-Sendable closures) adopted via two-tier architecture; F3 (sync mutation + deferred resumption) adopted and structurally enforced via Decision/Action enums; P4 (nonisolated(nonsending)) fully adopted in primitives, deprecated #isolation pattern in 4 swift-async concrete operators; P7/P9 adopted and exceeded in primitives. 1 actionable recommendation: migrate #isolation to nonisolated(nonsending) in Async.Map/Filter/CompactMap/FlatMap. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

TCA26 isolation patterns applied to swift-kernel, swift-kernel-primitives, and platform packages (Darwin, Linux, POSIX, CPU, System). Per-pattern analysis: F2 (non-Sendable closures) already correctly adopted -- all 8 @Sendable sites are genuinely boundary-crossing (thread spawn, continuation callback), withLock is non-Sendable; F3 (sync mutation + async collection) adopted in Executor runLoop; F1 (isolation unification) not applicable below isolation boundary; P8 (pointer state) adopted more extensively than TCA26 (syscall buffers, ring buffers); P9 (ownership) correctly more advanced (~Copyable Descriptor/Worker, ~Escapable Environment.Entry, consuming join, borrowing syscalls). 6 @unchecked Sendable types all justified. 0 actionable changes -- kernel layer is already clean. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

Temporal operator determinism (Tier 2) — NonsendingClock and deterministic temporal testing, triggered by Pointfree Episode #355. ImmediateClock with nonisolated(nonsending) sleep achieves 100% deterministic testing (10,000 runs, 0 failures).

documents.displayName: Tensor Primitives and Tensors Package Decomposition
documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.1.0, 2026-05-16): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide architectural decomposition for two new packages — `swift-tensor-primitives` at L1 (owns the Tensor value type and intrinsic operations) and `swift-tensors` at L3 (composes L1 + L2 specs + other L1s) — providing the institute's runtime-shape n-dimensional array surface as a complement to the existing compile-time-shape `Linear.Matrix<Rows, Columns>` / `Linear.Vector<N>` primitives. v1.1.0 supersedes v1.0.0 on three dimensions: (1) layer-placement correction — v1.0.0 incorrectly placed the Tensor concrete type at L3; corrected to L1 per [ECO-003] (L1 owns irreducible types; L3 composes), matching every existing L1 package (Buffer.Aligned, Linear.Matrix<R,C>, Complex.Number<Scalar>, Tagged<Tag, Underlying>); (2) strict /code-surface pass — `Tensor.MutableView` (compound name [API-NAME-002] violation) corrected to `Tensor.View.Mutable`; typed throws [API-ERR-001] made explicit on every fallible operation; Swift.Error qualification [PLAT-ARCH-011]; hoisted-protocol pattern [API-IMPL-009] for Tensor.Layout.Protocol and Tensor.Axis.Protocol; one-type-per-file [API-IMPL-005] file layout enumerated; (3) strict /modularization + /platform pass — Core internal [MOD-001] + umbrella-`@_exported`-only [MOD-005] + Test Support spine [MOD-024] + fine-grained per-type default [MOD-026]; SE-0450 trait-gated cross-package conformance [MOD-014] for Linear.Matrix interop; full Swift 6.3+ SwiftSetting profile [PATTERN-005/006/007]; L1 platform-agnostic [PLAT-ARCH-008c]; L3 libm via L2 swift-iso-9899 [PLAT-ARCH-008j]. Analysis sections preserved verbatim from v1.0.0 per [RES-008]: Step 0 internal grep, SLR (Kitchenham), Prior Art Survey (seven parallel subagent surveys: NumPy + ndarray, PyTorch + TensorFlow, JAX + MLX, Eigen + xtensor, ggml + apple/swift-numerics, theoretical grounding, internal ecosystem reuse), Theoretical Grounding (multilinear algebra / monoidal categories / Dex / SE-0393/0398/0452 reach), Formal Semantics (T-INDEX, T-MULTI-INDEX, T-SLICE, T-RESHAPE, T-BROADCAST, T-EINSUM typing rules + soundness invariant), Ecosystem Reuse Inventory (forty-plus primitive packages with file:line citations). [RES-018] case (b) domain-owned vocabulary at L1. [RES-021] contextualization step performed per universal pattern. [RES-023] empirical-claim verification with file:line/URL citations dated 2026-05-16. [RES-027] six premises (verification spikes pre-implementation: InlineArray + ~Copyable interop, Memory.Alignment.alignUp signature, Affine arithmetic on stride types, Tensor.Layout witness compilation, ~Copyable/~Escapable view lifetime-bound, SE-0398 variadic pack for Tensor.Named) + seven directions (element-type enum vs generic, reshape semantics, lazy/eager toggle, autograd, einsum surface, sparse/ragged variants, Tensor.Storage as type vs witness). Strategic Swift position: value-generic `Rank: Int` (SE-0452) for compile-time rank, runtime shape/stride values, Dex-style named-axis overlay via type-packs (SE-0398) as opt-in variant. L1 swift-tensor-primitives target structure: Tensor Primitives Core (internal — namespace + addressing types + Layout/Broadcast/Storage witnesses + Tensor value type + Tensor.View + Tensor.View.Mutable + intrinsic operations + typed errors), Tensor Dynamic Primitives (rank-erased variant), Tensor Named Primitives (Dex overlay variant), Tensor Primitives (umbrella), Tensor Primitives Test Support. L3 swift-tensors target structure: Tensors Core (internal — libm bridge via L2 swift-iso-9899), Tensors Operations (transcendental element-wise + reductions + matmul), Tensors FFT (Cooley-Tukey + Bluestein), Tensors Einsum (parser + executor), Tensors Linear (SE-0450 trait-gated Linear.Matrix/Vector interop), Tensors Format (Format.Tensor style), Tensors (umbrella), Tensors Test Support. L2 swift-dlpack-standard deferred until concrete L4 consumer needs DLPack interop. L4 platform compute backends (Accelerate/MPS/BLAS) out of scope. Implementation gated on principal authorization. Skill-Conformance Audit Annex enumerates per-rule conformance for /code-surface ([API-NAME-001..014, API-ERR-001..006, API-IMPL-005..019]), /implementation ([IMPL-INTENT, IMPL-COMPILE, IMPL-000..108, PATTERN-009+]), /modularization ([MOD-DOMAIN, MOD-001..029, MOD-RENT]), /platform ([PLAT-ARCH-008c, 008j, 011, 022, PATTERN-001..008]).
documents.statusDetail: v1.2.0 (2026-05-17). Tier 3 ecosystem-wide. v1.2.0 promoted RECOMMENDATION → IMPLEMENTED on completion of L1 swift-tensor-primitives at /Users/coen/Developer/swift-primitives/swift-tensor-primitives/. Three doc-level amendments captured: (1) Layout naming refined from Tensor.Layout.RowMajor/ColumnMajor (v1.1.0; [API-NAME-001] violation — compound type names) to Tensor.Layout.Order.Row/Order.Column (v1.2.0; nested under Order sub-namespace; Strided retained as sibling, not member). Prior art survey covered NumPy 'C'/'F', C++23 mdspan layout_left/right/stride, BLAS CblasRowMajor/ColMajor, Eigen, ndarray; nested-Order option selected on combined institute-rule + term-of-art + readability axes. (2) Three structural deviations stamped (Swift generic-arity constraint forced these): Tensor<Element,Rank,Layout> → Tensor.Value<Element,Rank,Layout>; Tensor.Dynamic<Element> → Tensor.Dynamic.Value<Element>+Tensor.Dynamic.Shape; Tensor.Index<Rank> → Tensor.Index.Position<Rank>+Tensor.Index.Error. (3) swift-vector-primitives added to L1 deps for typed-throws-aware iteration (stdlib Range<Int>.forEach doesn't preserve typed throws). Implementation outcome: 51 source files, 5 SwiftPM targets, swift build clean, swift test 21/21 passing, swift-linter 0 findings (initial 207 → cleanup 5 → second-opinion remediation 0). Repository remains private and untagged (no .git directory) per principal directive. L3 swift-tensors not yet implemented; remains the next dispatch. v1.1.0 supersession scope: Layer Decomposition, SwiftPM Target/Product Decomposition, Outcome, Audit Annex naming references. v1.0.0/v1.1.0 analysis sections preserved verbatim per [RES-008]. Companion artifact tensor-primitives-implementation-follow-ups.md v1.0.0 captures three skill-amendment candidates + Vector forEach ceremony gap + cross-package observation, all DEFERRED.

documents.topic: Test console output: from functional to elegant (Tier 2) — comprehensive analysis of console output across unit tests, performance benchmarks, and snapshot tests. Current state: flat streaming with no hierarchy, no suite aggregation, mixed formatting vocabularies. Apple: experimental AdvancedConsoleOutputRecorder with deferred tree rendering. Prior art: Jest, criterion.rs, hyperfine. Theoretical ideal: 6-phase plan — suite-level aggregation + tree summary (Phase 1), right-aligned durations + terminal width (Phase 2), box-drawn performance tables (Phase 3), real-time suite progress (Phase 4), side-by-side benchmark comparison (Phase 5), comparison CLI (Phase 6). Hybrid reporter architecture (Option C) recommended.

documents.topic: Trie and keyed-tree SLR (Tier 3) — Kitchenham methodology, 34 sources across 7 languages. RQ1: canonical signature `Trie<K,V>` where key = `[K]`. RQ2: tries classified as maps, never trees. RQ3: Hinze exponent-law derivation. RQ4: keyed trees extremely rare (Boost.PropertyTree only). Child representation taxonomy (7 strategies).

DISPOSITIONED (v1.1.1, 2026-05-20): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide audit closing the verify-before-claim phase of the canada-anomaly v1.4.1 Pre-Gate 1 finding. Four unspecialized sites (Cursor.peek / Lexer.Scanner.peek<X: Byte.Protocol> / Tagged.retag<A> / _CarrierProtocol.vector witness) audited against six annotations (@inlinable / @frozen / @usableFromInline / public import / @_alwaysEmitIntoClient / @_transparent). Phase 1 audit identified annotation gaps; Phase 2 applied mechanical fixes (3 commits across swift-carrier-primitives `c2849a3`, swift-cursor-primitives `e2da665`, swift-lexer-primitives `d2418f4`): @_alwaysEmitIntoClient on 9 Carrier.Protocol default-impl declarations (5 files) + @frozen on Cursor<DomainTag> + @frozen on Lexer.Scanner. Phase 3 SIL re-emit confirms gaps not closed: Cursor.peek(), Lexer.Scanner.peek<X>(), Tagged.retag<A> remain only as generic-only forms in the bench binary; Cursor.consume() (same extension, same annotations) IS specialized as `generic specialization <serialized, Text_Primitives.Text>`. The asymmetry signals compiler-side specialization heuristic skipping Optional-returning members under otherwise-identical conditions. Phase 4 wall-clock null delta (236→241 ms vs 235.62 baseline, within ±5 ms noise). Tagged.init(_unchecked:) @inlinable BLOCKED on Swift language limitation: @inlinable cannot reference package-scope or internal-scope setter; @usableFromInline on stored-property declaration does not lift SETTER visibility. Three resolution paths for Tagged were surfaced: (a) widen setter to public — loses package-set encapsulation; (b) move to explicit getter/setter — loses stored-shape that the docstring calls 'load-bearing' for Carrier protocol + partial-consume of ~Copyable Underlying; (c) accept language limitation, treat as compiler-bug material. **Principal chose path (c)** 2026-05-20 — consistent with the parallel CONFIRMED-COMPILER-BUG verdicts on Cursor.peek and Lexer.Scanner.peek<X> (which are unspecialized despite full annotation hygiene). Final verdict: 3 of 4 sites CONFIRMED-COMPILER-BUG (Cursor.peek, Lexer.Scanner.peek<X>, Tagged.retag<A>); 4th site (Carrier defaults) FIXED but doesn't reach canada hot path. NO upstream filing in this dispatch (re-confirmed 2026-05-20: filing requires explicit YES per principal).
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.1 (2026-05-20). Tier 3 ecosystem-wide per [RES-020]. Carrier-primitives @_alwaysEmitIntoClient + Cursor @frozen + Lexer.Scanner @frozen all LANDED (3 commits across swift-carrier-primitives c2849a3, swift-cursor-primitives e2da665, swift-lexer-primitives d2418f4). Tagged dispositioned via path (c) — accept language limitation; preserves package(set) encapsulation + stored-shape semantic; Tagged.retag<A> joins the CONFIRMED-COMPILER-BUG column alongside the peek family. Per [RES-019] internal grep performed; no existing research covers this cross-module specialization pattern. Per [RES-023] every file:line citation verified at write time. documents.topic: Typed throws mixed error domains (Tier 1) — 4 options for aggregate functions composing multiple typed-throws sub-operations: unified error enum (A), Either/sum type (B), phase-tagged wrapping (C), foreign re-throw (D). 3 concrete sites: rfc-9112 Request/Response deserializers (6 error types each), iso-8601 DateTime (1 foreign Time.Error). Recommends Option A (unified error enum) for all sites — wrapping sub-phase errors as associated values preserves full type safety, readability, layer isolation.

documents.displayName: UInt8 Elimination — Phase 0 Inventory & Gap Analysis
documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-25): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide Phase 0 inventory + gap analysis for the UInt8 elimination program (confining UInt8 to genuinely structurally-forced sites; everything else → Byte / ASCII.Code / Unicode.UTF{8,16,32}.CodeUnit / Path.Char per the substrate matrix). Precedes any lint-rule-promotion arc. Workspace-wide rg across 7 active org dirs (swift-iec empty): 5,867 raw UInt8 source lines / 944 files; 2,416 (41%) in Tests/, 1,089 (19%) comment-leading; 2,540 clean (source, non-test, non-comment) migratable universe. Per-layer clean Byte:UInt8 progress proxy — L1 800:636 (Byte-majority), L2 805:1146, L3 202:758 — confirms substitution advanced at L1 and thinned up-stack. Four ask conditions adjudicated: #1 (>200/pkg) YES on clean basis for swift-iso-32000 (385) + swift-ascii-primitives (360) → each a multi-wave program (swift-ascii 600 raw→165 clean and swift-binary-primitives 251 raw→25 clean do NOT exceed on the migratable basis); #2 (Byte shift-API cascade >100) NO — rhs:UInt8 shift count in 2 operator defs (Byte.Protocol+Bitwise.swift:70,80), 0 explicit consumer sites, cheap optional enabler; #3 (substrate-matrix gap) YES — 31 legacy single-byte PDF codecs in swift-iso-32000 (WinAnsi/MacRoman/CP1252/etc., Scalar↔byte over non-Unicode code pages) fit neither BYTE nor CODEC (matrix scopes CODEC to UTF-8/16/32), plus ~13 IETF struct{rawValue:UInt8} wire carriers needing [API-BYTE-004] per-type disposition; #4 (AST-detectable <70%) YES decisively — only ~8-10% of clean sites mechanically classifiable (carrier/SLI paths, @_disfavoredOverload, enum/OptionSet syntax, typed pointer/buffer), ~90% require domain judgment AST cannot resolve (same limit as [API-BYTE-004] + [MOD-016]). Aggregate clean classes: BYTE ~900, ASCII ~385, FORCED-ARITH ~576, FORCED-STDLIB ~105, FORWARDER ~75, FORCED-ENUM ~50, FORCED-CABI ~27 (concentrated in iso-9945, walled in ABI.CChar), CODEUNIT ~22, CODEC ~21, AMBIGUOUS ~56. Empirically verified (swiftc -typecheck /tmp spike) that enum X: Byte is type-system-viable (a struct with Byte's conformance set is a legal raw-value-enum raw type) — so 'enum rawValue → UInt8 stays' is an idiom not a language force (except OptionSet: Byte ≢ FixedWidthInteger, hard-forced); 26 rawValue:Byte sites already exist as precedent. Four subprograms sized: (1) Byte shift signature ~2 sites; (2) swift-ascii inventory 532 clean across 3 pkgs, real work = reconcile dual ASCII-constant surfaces + digit/case arithmetic placement; (3) C-ABI contained (iso-9945 ~20 sites, boundary-rebind pattern, no propagation anti-pattern; filename C-ABI already scheduled via the filename-storage program); (4) enum/struct rawValue principal-disposition. RECOMMENDATION: PULL BACK to gradual / migration-first — an aggressive 'forbid UInt8 by default' rule would fire on the ~2,300-site semantic residual with high false-positive density and replay the [MOD-016] revert; sequence = optional shift enabler → principal dispositions → per-package BYTE migration (swift-iso-32000 '3.7 byte.swift:26 typealias Byte = UInt8' repoint is highest-leverage) → ASCII program → narrow residual stamp-rule last. Five open questions (OQ1-OQ5) surfaced for principal disposition.

Tier 2 ecosystem-wide prior-art survey (parallel-subagent verified per [RES-020]) of Swift Wasm CI strategy after γ-3's commit `8e3a76c` rollout surfaced a 6.3.0-SDK ↔ 6.3.1-compiler ABI mismatch. Six sub-questions: (Q1) SDK distribution model — swift.org canonical since Swift 6.2 (2025-09-15); every 6.2.x and 6.3.x patch has a Wasm SDK with checksum exposed via `https://www.swift.org/api/v1/install/releases.json`; no published guarantee but strong empirical pattern; community swiftwasm/swift-sdk-index has gaps (no 6.3.1) and is not a viable canonical source; swiftly does not yet handle Wasm SDKs. (Q2) Embedded vs Wasi disambiguation — three concepts: the `-enable-experimental-feature Embedded` compiler flag, the `_wasm` SDK ID (full stdlib + WASI), and the `_wasm-embedded` SDK ID. CRITICAL FINDING: both SDK IDs target the SAME `wasm32-unknown-wasip1` triple; embedded is NOT bare-metal; the `embedded-toolset.json` automatically passes `-enable-experimental-feature Embedded -static-stdlib -wmo -D__EMBEDDED_SWIFT__` to swiftc when `_wasm-embedded` is selected. The `_wasm-embedded` variant is the closer fit for swift-primitives' L1 freestanding-buildability invariant. (Q3) Stable-vs-nightly framing in v1.1.0 §3.4.6 was based on incomplete swift-syntax read — the actual swift-syntax Wasm matrix is `["nightly-main", "nightly-6.3", "6.2"]` (or 6.3 in newer reusable versions), both stable AND nightly together, hard-gated. 6 of 7 surveyed Apple packages (foundation/collections/numerics/system/async-algorithms/argument-parser) use the swiftlang/github-workflows reusable; swift-nio is the lone holdout with bespoke `wasm_swift_sdk.yml`. ALL hard-gated; only swift-collections uses the embedded variant alongside wasi. (Q4) Job structure — three options analyzed: α status quo + dynamic resolution (RECOMMENDED), β adopt swiftlang reusable as sub-job, γ full migration. Recommendation: Option α — replace hardcoded SDK URL+checksum with curl/python3 lookup against `releases.json` (sketch provided). Keeps two-job shape (`embedded` Linux nightly + `embedded-wasm-sdk`); do NOT add wasi-variant job at L1 (does not test L1 invariant). (Q5) Apple precedent — Wasm CI is now table stakes; dominant pattern is the swiftlang reusable; Institute starting advisory is more conservative than any Apple package; destination state should match industry hard-gating. (Q6) Soak taxonomy — extend class C with C.1 (install network/availability) vs C.2 (SDK ↔ toolchain ABI mismatch); §3.5.7's incident reclassifies B → C.2; if Option α is adopted, C.2 becomes empty by construction. Open follow-ups: per-L1-package empirical embedded-on-Wasm-WASI verification, L2/L3 layer wrappers' future Wasm decisions, swiftly Wasm-SDK support, Embedded compiler-flag stabilization.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Tier 2 prior-art survey produced via parallel-subagent verification per [RES-020] against primary sources: swift.org docs + API endpoints, swiftlang/swift-syntax + swiftlang/github-workflows + 7 Apple swift-* packages on GitHub, the official Wasm artifactbundle's info.json/swift-sdk.json/embedded-swift-sdk.json/toolset.json/embedded-toolset.json (extracted), forums.swift.org Wasm monthly digests March + April 2026 + the June 2025 announcement, Swift Evolution Embedded vision document. Recommendation: adopt dynamic SDK resolution from `releases.json` (single-commit edit to `swift-primitives/.github/.github/workflows/swift-ci.yml` `embedded-wasm-sdk` job's `Install Embedded Wasm SDK` step) — implementation gated per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes. Keep `_wasm-embedded` SDK variant (matches L1 freestanding-buildability invariant; non-suffixed `_wasm` would not). Keep two-job shape; do not add wasi-variant at L1. Maintain γ-3 advisory through 4-week soak; flip to hard-gate to match Apple ecosystem default. Extend soak taxonomy with C.1/C.2 sub-class; recommend §3.5.7 incident be reclassified B → C.2 in centralized doc's next revision. No code change executed in this Phase 1 (research-only per parent handoff scope).

documents.topic: Empirical survey of five candidate naming patterns for the Windows-side L2/L3 platform stack, triggered by Path X close (2026-04-30) and the user direction that future strict-platform compliance phases raw forms out at L2 entirely (typed-only L2 surface). Five variants tested as 3-target SPM shapes: V1 status-quo (Windows.Kernel.X at both L2 and L3), V2 sub-namespace (Windows.ABI.Kernel.X at L2), V3 twin roots (Win32.Kernel.X at L2, mirrors POSIX ISO_9945/POSIX shape), V4 org-prefix (Microsoft.Kernel.X at L2), V5 literal-spec (WinSDK.Kernel.X at L2). All 16 targets build green; differential signal is architectural. Recommends V3 (twin roots) on spec/policy-separation, [API-NAME-003] specification-mirroring, and POSIX-shape-symmetry grounds. Surfaces the open question of whether the existing POSIX = ISO_9945 typealias should break for cross-platform symmetry (principal decision; not autonomously decided).
documents.statusDetail: Recommendation: V3 twin roots (Win32.Kernel.X at L2 + Windows.Kernel.X at L3). V1 REFUTED architecturally (no spec/policy boundary; future typed-only L2 collision). V2 sub-optimal (ABI sub-tag is not a Microsoft-published spec authority). V4 REFUTED (org-prefix root contradicts [API-NAME-003] spec-mirroring; ecosystem already roots specs at the spec name, not the publisher). V5 REFUTED (Windows-host-only module-vs-type collision invisible to macOS/Linux CI). Open question on POSIX-side typealias-break surfaced for principal decision.

DECISION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-18): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide DECISION closing HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 3 / triage R3. Question: should the institute introduce Word16.Protocol / Word32.Protocol / Word64.Protocol as L1 capability-marker protocols, parallel to Byte.Protocol? Verdict: **Principled refuse**. Two independent rules dispose of the proposal. (1) [RES-018] case (a) — the proposal originates from symmetric-completeness reasoning ('Byte.Protocol exists, so by analogy Word16.Protocol should exist'); no concrete cross-domain consumer surfaces. Hypothetical candidates (UTF16.CodeUnit, Unicode.Scalar) work fine today as concrete types without capability-marker conformance. (2) [API-NAME-001c] anti-meta-protocol verdict — even if consumers materialized, the principled response would be per-domain markers (e.g., a UTF16.CodeUnit-specific marker), NOT unified Word*.Protocol. Closes Wave 1 Item 3; revisit conditions documented (concrete cross-domain consumer needing word-size capability-marker AND domains spanning unrelated enough that per-domain markers fail — both unlikely).
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0 (2026-05-18, DECISION). No source changes. Closes HANDOFF.md Wave 1 Item 3 / triage R3. Principal in-chat decision 2026-05-18 per first-principles ground rule (settle or principled-refuse, no ambiguous defer). Cited by [RES-018] case (a) precedent + [API-NAME-001c] per-domain marker discipline.

documents.topic: Phase 1 deployment-readiness analysis for the three deterministic γ-class advisory reusable workflows specified in `centralized-swift-ci-and-spine-gate.md` v1.2.0 §3.4.2 / §3.4.3 / §3.4.5: γ-1a Foundation-family import enforcement, γ-1b License-header advisory (Stage 1 of three), γ-2 Mechanical hygiene (YAML lint + broken-symlink, separate reusables per §3.4.11). Originating dispatch (`HANDOFF-workflow-construction-phase-1.md` 2026-05-05) framed phase 1 as BUILD; on inspection, the four reusables (`lint-foundation-family-import.yml` + `lint-license-header.yml` + `lint-yaml.yml` + `lint-broken-symlink.yml`) had already landed (commits `ea4128f`, `6c3d726`, `2480628`), conformed to v1.3.0 §3.5 corrections (advisory-as-input pattern, ubuntu-latest no-container, shell:bash explicit), and were canaried green on swift-carrier-primitives + swift-tagged-primitives. RECOMMENDATION (v1.2.0, Tier 2): contract-first analysis of swift-collection-primitives@43d1fb7 making Collection.`Protocol`.Index a `Comparison.`Protocol` & ~Escapable`-admitting associatedtype. Quantified fallout: true blast radius is input-primitives' Input.Slice (5 files); dictionary/infinite/queue break only transitively via their input dep; array/cache/heap/slab/stack are the separate Sequence-rename (W1) arc. v1.2.0 (principal's methodological correction): current-consumer breakage is a TRANSITIONAL ARTIFACT and does NOT vote — decide contract-first. Frame: `& ~Escapable` is a suppression admitting both Escapable and ~Escapable conformers; axis is KEEP (permissive base) vs REQUIRE Escapable (restrictive); associatedtype kept either way. Supply-side fact: ZERO ~Escapable-index conformers (KEEP is speculative admission), while custom Escapable index domains (Int, Tagged<_,Ordinal>, Array.Index) have real supply (associatedtype justified). Both-ways steelman + forward impact vs the Collection/Sequence detachment decision (which separates on access-mode, used a concrete Escapable index, doesn't settle storability). Recommendation REQUIRE Escapable (keep associatedtype, drop ~Escapable): an index's defining capability over a cursor is exportability (Range/slice/return/checkpoint all need escape); V4 ~Copyable-Index REFUTED shows Index is contract-bearing not payload like Element; a non-exportable index is a Sequence cursor with a home in the iterator tier. Updates the v1.1.0 (a)-narrow lean (which rested on the excluded consumer-cost). KEEP-vs-REQUIRE token remains the principal's. Independent of W1. Pending principal confirmation (do not execute).
documents.statusDetail: v1.3.0 — PRINCIPAL DECISION: KEEP ~Escapable on Collection.Protocol.Index; 43d1fb7 STANDS. Escapable is an operation-level requirement (escape-operations pin it), not intrinsic; Copyable floor via V4. Supersedes the v1.2.0 REQUIRE recommendation. Consumer migration (Input.Slice pin where Base.Index == Index<Base.Element>) is ATOMIC across its conformance set, linchpin = the Collection.Protocol conformance in input's Input.Slice+Collection.Slice.Protocol.swift, currently held by the World-B chat (Sequence-rename WIP, build-consistent). Empirically: partial pinning (4 of ~5 files) INCREASES errors. So the migration needs one coherent commit by whoever owns input's tree — pending coordination/sequencing (principal's call). Not yet implemented.

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-30): Tier 2 cross-package. Web-sourced prior-art survey on whether Buffer.Protocol should expose its underlying STORAGE as an associated TYPE (`associatedtype Storage` + `var storage: Storage`) so a generic `extension Iterable where Self: Buffer.Protocol` vends a borrow-iterator for all buffers. Taxonomy: T1 storage-as-associated-type / T2 allocator-strategy-param / T3 scoped-accessor / T4 contiguous-as-capability-concept. VERDICT: T1 is NOVEL / anti-precedented among stabilized ecosystems — NO mainstream stdlib exposes the storage object as a member TYPE on a buffer/collection abstraction. Swift = T3 (`withContiguousStorageIfAvailable`) + T4 (`Span`); SE-0447 explicitly DEFERRED a `ContiguousStorage` protocol (can't suppress Copyable on associatedtype; can't put `_read` in a protocol requirement — the SAME wall the Institute hit) and SE-0256 (ContiguousCollection) was REJECTED. Rust = T2 (`Vec<T, A: Allocator>` strategy) + T4 (`Deref<Target=[T]>`/`as_slice` slice-view capability); the only T1 instance (Storage API pre-RFC, opaque `Handle` not pointer) is UNSTABILIZED after 2+ yrs. C++ = T2 (allocator/PMR) + T4 (C++20 `contiguous_range` concept requiring `ranges::data(t)` yields a pointer — contiguity-as-concept, the canonical modern form). Zig = T2 explicit allocator. Python PEP 3118 = T3 (`Py_buffer` base pointer via scoped GetBuffer/Release lifecycle, borrowed-not-stored). Adversarial: no stabilized ecosystem does T1 due to (1) encapsulation/ABI-evolution, (2) escaping-base aliasing hazard (Swift Span + Python Py_buffer both DELIBERATELY scope the base), (3) the `_read`/~Copyable wall is Swift signaling T1 is the wrong shape, not a defect to route around. RECOMMENDATION: do NOT add `associatedtype Storage` to Buffer.Protocol; adopt T4 — keep Buffer.Protocol = logical occupancy (count/isEmpty) HAS-A storage by composition, vend the borrow-iterator from a contiguous-read CAPABILITY (Memory.Contiguous.Protocol span / generic-over-`some Storage.Protocol`) composed `where Self: <capability>`. This MATCHES + is governed by ([RES-019]) the APPROVED 2026-05-28 cross-layer-capability-protocol-model (Buffer.Protocol 'does NOT refine Storage.Protocol (has-a)'; span on Memory.Contiguous.Protocol); the T1 proposal would CONTRADICT that APPROVED Tier-3 decision. Primary sources block-quoted + [Verified: 2026-05-30] per [RES-020]/[RES-026].
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0 (2026-05-30). Tier 2 per [RES-020], cross-package. Web-sourced prior-art survey. Verdict: storage-as-associated-type (T1) is novel/anti-precedented; dominant pattern is contiguous-as-capability (T4) + allocator-strategy param (T2) + scoped accessor (T3). Aligns with and governed by the APPROVED cross-layer-capability-protocol-model (Buffer.Protocol does NOT refine Storage.Protocol). Primary sources verified [Verified: 2026-05-30].

documents.displayName: Iterable ↔ SE-0516 Alignment — the Span-Primitive Realignment
documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-30): Tier 2 cross-package. Precise SE-0516 "Iterable (formerly BorrowingSequence)" ↔ institute mapping + the aligned span-primitive Iterable design + the delta from current. CORE FINDING (verified against the local swiftlang/swift clone): institute Iterable is SCALAR-primitive (its iterator is the move-out Iterator.Protocol.next() -> Element?, Copyable-gated), whereas SE-0516 is SPAN-primitive (nextSpan -> Span<Element>, no scalar next()); Span<Element: ~Copyable> (Span.swift:29) means ONE span-iterator serves BOTH element kinds. The institute already ships the structural machinery (Iterator.Chunk ≅ SpanIterator; __IteratorChunkProtocol ≅ BorrowingIteratorProtocol) — alignment is re-pointing + bound-relaxation, not invention. The institute Iterator.Chunk over-narrows Element to Escapable (__IteratorChunkProtocol.swift:35) when Span only requires ~Copyable; relaxing that + re-pointing Iterable.Iterator from scalar Iterator.Protocol to the span __IteratorChunkProtocol makes one iterator serve both kinds, dissolves the Copyable-gate on Iterable, dissolves the dual-conformer/element-kind split on buffers + Set.Ordered (the memory→Iterable bridge relaxes Copyable→~Copyable, vending Iterator.Chunk over span). forEach: keep the terminal suite but rebuild on the span loop (for span; for i { body(span[i]) }) — works both kinds, no Copyable gate, subsumes the bespoke Memory.Contiguous floor. ITERATOR.BORROW DISPOSITION: UNNECESSARY FOR ITERATION (the span model covers ~Copyable via Span borrowing access; SE-0516 explicitly REJECTED the Ownership.Borrow-style element-wrapping that Iterator.Borrow.Scalar implements, on call-site-ergonomics grounds) — reverses unified-iteration-design.md §2.5 Option C; but PARK not delete (f31ce11/dd60699 stay on disk per [ARCH-LAYER-009] no-pre-1.0-removal; possible non-iteration cursor role). This RECOMMENDATION proposes a DELTA to the APPROVED unified-iteration-design.md §2.1/§2.3/§2.5/§3 (replacing the §2.5 Option C keystone) — principal must ratify. One soundness item flagged as build-verify gate (Iterator.Chunk(span) over inline @_rawLayout storage for ~Copyable). SE-0516 is Returned-for-Revision (volatility risk tracked): align to the shipped-clone Element: ~Copyable, treat the revision-pitch ~Copyable & ~Escapable relaxation as unreconciled aspiration; institute name-independence insulates against upstream naming churn.

Proposes a delta to the APPROVED unified-iteration-design.md (principal ratifies). All SE-0516 facts Verified 2026-05-30 against the local swiftlang/swift clone (BorrowingSequence.swift, Span/Span.swift); all institute claims cited file:line.
